questions.question: Explain the following    * Availability zone   * Region   * Edge location
questions.answer:
AWS regions are data centers hosted across different geographical locations worldwide.

Within each region, there are multiple isolated locations known as Availability Zones. Each availability zone is one or more data-centers with redundant network and connectivity and power supply. Multiple availability zones ensure high availability in case one of them goes down.

Edge locations are basically content delivery network which caches data and insures lower latency and faster delivery to the users in any location. They are located in major cities in the world.

questions.question: True or False? Each AWS region is designed to be completely isolated from the other AWS regions
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: True or False? Each region has a minimum number of 1 availability zones and the maximum is 4
questions.answer: False. The minimum is 2 while the maximum is 6.

questions.question: What considerations to take when choosing an AWS region for running a new application?
questions.answer:
* Services Availability: not all service (and all their features) are available in every region
* Reduced latency: deploy application in a region that is close to customers
* Compliance: some countries have more strict rules and requirements such as making sure the data stays within the borders of the country or the region. In that case, only specific region can be used for running the application
* Pricing: the pricing might not be consistent across regions so, the price for the same service in different regions might be different.

questions.question: What is IAM? What are some of its features?
questions.answer:
IAM (Identity and Access Management) is AWS's identity management service used to control access to AWS resources securely. Its core components:

* **Users** ŌĆö Individual people or applications with long-term credentials
* **Groups** ŌĆö Collections of users for bulk permission assignment
* **Roles** ŌĆö Temporary credentials for services or cross-account access (uses STS)
* **Policies** ŌĆö JSON documents defining allowed/denied actions on specific resources

Key features:
* **Global service** ŌĆö Not region-scoped; one IAM for the entire account
* **Fine-grained permissions** ŌĆö Control down to individual API actions
* **Federation support** ŌĆö SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect for corporate SSO
* **Free** ŌĆö IAM itself has no additional cost (only the resources it governs may cost)

­¤ō¢ **Docs:**
* IAM Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/iam/
* IAM Best Practices: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html
* IAM FAQs: https://aws.amazon.com/iam/faqs/

­¤ö¦ **CLI Quickstart:**
```bash
# List all IAM users in the account
aws iam list-users

# List custom (customer-managed) policies
aws iam list-policies --scope Local

# Check which groups a user belongs to
aws iam list-groups-for-user --user-name Alice

# Get details of a specific policy
aws iam get-policy --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
```

questions.question: True or False? IAM configuration is defined globally and not per region
questions.answer:
True. IAM is a global service ŌĆö users, groups, roles, and policies are available across all AWS regions automatically.

This means:
* A user created in IAM can access services in any region (subject to their policy)
* IAM data is physically replicated across AWS infrastructure
* Unlike regional services (EC2, RDS, S3 buckets), you don't select a region when working with IAM

Other global AWS services include: Route 53, CloudFront, WAF, and AWS Organizations.

­¤ö¦ **Verify it yourself:**
```bash
# These return identical results regardless of --region
aws iam list-users --region us-east-1
aws iam list-users --region ap-southeast-1
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/introduction.html

questions.question: True or False? When creating an AWS account, root account is created by default. This is the recommended account to use and share in your organization
questions.answer:
False. The root account (created with your email) has unrestricted access to everything in the account. AWS strongly recommends:

* **Never use the root account for daily tasks** ŌĆö create individual IAM users instead
* **Delete root access keys** if they exist
* **Enable MFA on the root account** ŌĆö this is the single most important security step
* **Lock away root credentials** ŌĆö only use them for tasks that require root (e.g., closing the account, changing payment methods)

­¤ö¦ **Check your root account security:**
```bash
# Get the account summary (MFA status, access keys on root, etc.)
aws iam get-account-summary

# List all IAM users (root account won't appear here)
aws iam list-users

# Generate a credential report to audit all users including root
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report --output text | base64 -d
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#lock-away-credentials

questions.question: True or False? Groups in AWS IAM, can contain only users and not other groups
questions.answer:
True. IAM groups can only contain users, not other groups (no nested groups).

Key points about IAM groups:
* Groups are simply a way to assign the same permissions to multiple users
* A group cannot be a member of another group
* Users can belong to up to 10 groups (default limit, can be increased)
* Groups cannot be identified as a Principal in a resource-based policy (only users and roles can)

­¤ÆĪ This is different from Active Directory or GCP IAM where group nesting is supported.

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# Create a group
aws iam create-group --group-name DevOps

# Add a user to a group
aws iam add-user-to-group --group-name DevOps --user-name Alice

# List members of a group
aws iam get-group --group-name DevOps
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_groups.html

questions.question: True or False? Users in AWS IAM, can belong only to a single group
questions.answer:
False. A user can belong to multiple IAM groups (up to 10 by default, though this is a soft limit).

A user's effective permissions are the **union** of all permissions from:
1. Policies directly attached to the user
2. Policies attached to any group the user belongs to
3. Permissions granted through roles the user assumes

A **Deny** always overrides an **Allow** ŌĆö if any policy explicitly denies an action, it's denied.

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# See all groups a user belongs to
aws iam list-groups-for-user --user-name Alice

# List all users and their group memberships
for user in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].UserName' --output text); do
  echo "=== $user ==="
  aws iam list-groups-for-user --user-name "$user" --query 'Groups[*].GroupName' --output text
done
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_groups.html

questions.question: What are some best practices regarding IAM in AWS?
questions.answer:
* **Lock away the root account** ŌĆö Delete root access keys, enable MFA, never use root for daily work
* **One human = one IAM user** ŌĆö Don't share user credentials across team members
* **Apply least privilege principle** ŌĆö Grant only the permissions needed for the job, nothing more. Start with minimal permissions and expand as needed
* **Use groups for permission management** ŌĆö Assign permissions to groups, then add users to groups (User ŌåÆ Group ŌåÆ Policy)
* **Enforce MFA** ŌĆö Require multi-factor authentication, especially for privileged users
* **Use IAM Roles for applications on EC2/Lambda/ECS** ŌĆö Never hardcode access keys in application code
* **Rotate access keys regularly** ŌĆö Automate key rotation for programmatic access
* **Enable IAM Access Analyzer** ŌĆö Identifies resources shared with external entities
* **Use permission boundaries** ŌĆö Set guardrails to limit the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have
* **Monitor with CloudTrail** ŌĆö Track IAM API calls for auditing and security analysis

­¤ö¦ **Quick security audit commands:**
```bash
# Check account security status (MFA, access keys age, etc.)
aws iam get-account-summary

# Find users with access keys older than 90 days
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name Alice --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[?CreateDate<`2025-01-01`]'

# List users who have never logged in to the console
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[?PasswordLastUsed==`null`].UserName' --output text

# Check if MFA is enabled for a user
aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name Alice
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html

questions.question: What permissions does a new user have?
questions.answer:
A newly created IAM user has **no permissions at all** ŌĆö they can only log in (if a password is set). This is the "deny by default" model.

Without explicit permissions (via group membership or directly attached policies), the user cannot:
* List or access any AWS resources
* Call any AWS API action
* Access the management console beyond the login page

This enforces the **principle of least privilege** ŌĆö you start with nothing and add only what's needed.

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Create and verify a new user:**
```bash
# Create a user (no permissions by default)
aws iam create-user --user-name Alice

# Try to list S3 buckets as this user (will fail with AccessDenied)
aws s3 ls --profile alice

# Attach an admin policy to the user
aws iam attach-user-policy \
  --user-name Alice \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Now the user can access everything
aws s3 ls --profile alice
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies.html

questions.question: True or False? If a user in AWS is using password for authenticating, he doesn't needs to enable MFA
questions.answer:
False! MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) adds a critical second layer beyond a password. A password alone is not enough ŌĆö if it's compromised, the attacker gets direct access. MFA requires something you know (password) + something you have (virtual MFA device, hardware key, or U2F security key).

AWS MFA options:
* **Virtual MFA** ŌĆö Authenticator apps like Google Authenticator, Authy (free)
* **Hardware MFA** ŌĆö Physical key fob such as Gemalto
* **U2F / Passkey** ŌĆö YubiKey or similar FIDO-compliant devices (supports multiple users per device)

­¤ÆĪ Account-level best practice: create an IAM policy that **requires** MFA for all console users and attach it to groups.

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Enforce MFA with a policy:**
```bash
# List MFA devices for a user (empty = no MFA)
aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name Alice

# Enable a virtual MFA device (simplified ŌĆö actual setup requires QR code scan)
aws iam create-virtual-mfa-device --virtual-mfa-device-name AliceMFA
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_mfa.html

questions.question: What ways are there to access AWS?
questions.answer:
There are four main ways to interact with AWS:

| Method | Description | Best For |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **AWS Management Console** | Web-based GUI at https://console.aws.amazon.com | Beginners, visual monitoring, quick actions |
| **AWS CLI** | Command-line tool (`aws` command) | Automation, scripting, DevOps workflows |
| **AWS SDK** | Language-specific libraries (Python/boto3, JS, Go, Java, etc.) | Application development |
| **IaC Tools** | CloudFormation, Terraform, Pulumi, CDK | Infrastructure provisioning |

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Install and configure:**
```bash
# Install AWS CLI v2 (macOS)
brew install awscli

# Install AWS CLI v2 (Linux)
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip && sudo ./aws/install

# Configure with your credentials
aws configure
# Enter: Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Default region, Output format (json/yaml/table)
```

­¤ö¦ **SDK example (Python/boto3):**
```python
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')
buckets = s3.list_buckets()
for bucket in buckets['Buckets']:
    print(bucket['Name'])
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:**
* CLI: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/
* SDKs: https://aws.amazon.com/developer/tools/

questions.question: What are Roles?
questions.answer:
An IAM role is an identity with permissions, like a user, but **without long-term credentials**. Instead, it provides **temporary credentials** via AWS STS (Security Token Service).

**Role vs User:**
| | IAM User | IAM Role |
|---|---|---|
| Credentials | Long-lived (access keys, password) | Temporary (STS token, max 36h) |
| Who uses it | One person / one application | Services, cross-account, federation |
| Key management | Rotate manually or via automation | AWS handles rotation automatically |

**Common scenarios:**
* **EC2 instance ŌåÆ S3** ŌĆö Attach a role to EC2, app uses temporary credentials automatically
* **Lambda ŌåÆ DynamoDB** ŌĆö Execution role grants Lambda permissions to read/write
* **Cross-account access** ŌĆö Role in account B trusts account A to assume it
* **AWS service acting on your behalf** ŌĆö E.g., CodeBuild needs permissions to push to ECR

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Create and assume a role:**
```bash
# Create a role for EC2 to access S3
aws iam create-role \
  --role-name EC2S3ReadOnly \
  --assume-role-policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  }'

# Attach S3 read-only managed policy to the role
aws iam attach-role-policy \
  --role-name EC2S3ReadOnly \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess

# List policies attached to a role
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name EC2S3ReadOnly
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html

questions.question: What are Policies?
questions.answer:
An IAM policy is a JSON document that defines permissions ŌĆö it says who can do what to which resources under what conditions.

**Policy types:**
| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **AWS Managed** | Created and maintained by AWS | `AdministratorAccess`, `AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess` |
| **Customer Managed** | Created by you, reusable across entities | `DevTeamEC2Policy` |
| **Inline** | Embedded directly on one user/group/role | One-off exception for a specific user |

**Evaluation logic:**
1. Default = **Deny** (implicit)
2. If any policy says **Allow** and none says **Deny** ŌåÆ Allowed
3. If any policy says **Deny** (explicit) ŌåÆ Denied (always wins)
4. Permissions are the **union** of all applicable policies

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# List all IAM policies in the account (AWS-managed + custom)
aws iam list-policies

# List only custom (customer-managed) policies
aws iam list-policies --scope Local

# Show the JSON document of a policy
aws iam get-policy-version \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess \
  --version-id v1

# Create a custom policy
aws iam create-policy \
  --policy-name S3ListOnly \
  --policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:ListAllMyBuckets", "s3:GetBucketLocation"],
      "Resource": "*"
    }]
  }'
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies.html

questions.question: A user is unable to access an s3 bucket. What might be the problem?
questions.answer:
Multiple layers can block S3 access. Check these in order:

1. **IAM Policy** ŌĆö Is there an explicit Allow on the user/group for the required S3 actions? Does an explicit Deny override it?
2. **S3 Bucket Policy** ŌĆö Does the bucket-level policy allow this IAM user/role? (Resource-based policies work alongside IAM policies)
3. **S3 Block Public Access** ŌĆö Are public access block settings on the bucket/account overriding?
4. **VPC Endpoint Policy (if applicable)** ŌĆö If accessing S3 via VPC Endpoint, does the endpoint policy allow this bucket?
5. **SCP (Service Control Policy)** ŌĆö Is there an organization-level SCP that denies S3 access?
6. **Permission Boundary** ŌĆö Does the user/role have a permission boundary limiting maximum permissions?

­¤ÆĪ **The evaluation is the intersection**: IAM Policy Ō£Ś Bucket Policy Ō£Ś SCP Ō£Ś Permission Boundary

­¤ö¦ **Troubleshooting commands:**
```bash
# Check if the user can access a specific bucket
aws s3 ls s3://target-bucket/ --profile problem-user

# Simulate permissions ŌĆö does this user have s3:ListBucket on the target?
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/problem-user \
  --action-names "s3:ListBucket" \
  --resource-arns "arn:aws:s3:::target-bucket"

# Check the bucket's policy
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket target-bucket

# Check the user's attached policies
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name problem-user

# Check if SCP or permission boundary is the issue
aws iam list-user-policies --user-name problem-user
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** 
* IAM Policy Simulator: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_testing-policies.html
* S3 Troubleshooting: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/troubleshooting.html

questions.question: What should you use to:    - Grant access between two services/resources?   - Grant user access to resources/services?
questions.answer:
* **Role** ŌĆö for granting access between two services/resources. E.g., an EC2 instance assumes a role to access S3. The role provides temporary credentials automatically rotated by AWS STS.

* **Policy** ŌĆö for granting user access to resources/services. Attach the policy directly to the user or (better practice) to a group the user belongs to.

­¤ÆĪ **Interview tip:** The key distinction is *who* vs *what*. A Role answers "who is allowed to act" (a service, a cross-account principal). A Policy answers "what actions are allowed on which resources."

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö See the difference in action:**
```bash
# Role: create for EC2 ŌåÆ S3 inter-service access
aws iam create-role --role-name MyEC2Role --assume-role-policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"Service":"ec2.amazonaws.com"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}'

# Policy: attach to a user to grant them access
aws iam attach-user-policy --user-name Alice --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:**
* IAM Roles: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html
* IAM Policies: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies.html

questions.question: What statements AWS IAM policies are consist of?
questions.answer:
An IAM policy statement consists of these elements:

| Element | Required? | Description |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Sid** | No | Statement ID ŌĆö a descriptive name for the statement |
| **Effect** | Yes | `Allow` or `Deny` ŌĆö what happens when conditions match |
| **Action** | Yes | List of AWS API actions, e.g., `s3:ListBucket`, `ec2:RunInstances` |
| **Resource** | Yes | Which resources the actions apply to (ARNs), or `*` for all |
| **Principal** | Only for resource-based policies | Who the policy applies to (user, role, account, service) |
| **Condition** | No | When the policy applies ŌĆö based on tags, IP ranges, time, MFA status, etc. |

**Policy types by identity:**
* **Identity-based** (attached to user/group/role) ŌĆö Principal is NOT needed (it's implied)
* **Resource-based** (attached to S3 bucket, SQS queue, etc.) ŌĆö Principal IS required

­¤ÆĪ **Condition examples:**
```json
"Condition": {
  "IpAddress": {"aws:SourceIp": "10.0.0.0/8"},       // from corporate VPN only
  "Bool": {"aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "true"},      // MFA must be active
  "StringEquals": {"aws:RequestedRegion": "us-east-1"} // restrict to one region
}
```

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Create a policy with conditions:**
```bash
aws iam create-policy --policy-name RestrictedS3Access --policy-document '{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Sid": "AllowS3FromOffice",
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["s3:ListBucket", "s3:GetObject"],
    "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"],
    "Condition": {
      "IpAddress": {"aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24"}
    }
  }]
}'
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements.html

questions.question: Explain the following policy:  ``` {     "Version": "2012-10-17",     "Statement": [         {             "Effect:": "Allow",             "Action": "*",             "Resources": "*"         }     ] } ```
questions.answer:
This is the **AdministratorAccess** policy ŌĆö grants full access to all AWS services and resources.

Breakdown:
* `"Version": "2012-10-17"` ŌĆö The IAM policy language version (not a date you can change; 2008-10-17 is the older alternative)
* `"Effect": "Allow"` ŌĆö This is a permissive statement (vs `"Deny"`)
* `"Action": "*"` ŌĆö ALL actions on ALL services
* `"Resource": "*"` ŌĆö ALL resources across the AWS account

ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **Important: **`*` does NOT include IAM actions by default. Writing/deleting IAM entities (users, roles, policies) requires explicit IAM permissions. This policy grants `*` so it DOES include IAM actions ŌĆö making it one of the most powerful policies.

­¤ÆĪ **Best practice:** Avoid using this policy. Use it only for emergency break-glass roles with strict monitoring. For daily use, grant only the specific services and actions needed.

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# Find all entities (users, groups, roles) with this policy attached
aws iam list-entities-for-policy \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Attach this policy to a user (USE WITH CAUTION)
aws iam attach-user-policy \
  --user-name EmergencyAdmin \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_understand-policy-summaries.html

questions.question: What security tools AWS IAM provides?
questions.answer:
AWS IAM offers several security tools for auditing and compliance:

| Tool | Purpose | Scope |
|------|---------|-------|
| **IAM Credential Report** | CSV download showing all users + credential status (password age, access key age, MFA status) | Account-level |
| **IAM Access Advisor** | Shows which AWS services a user/role has accessed and when (last accessed info) | Per user/role |
| **IAM Access Analyzer** | Identifies resources shared with external entities (S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, etc.) and flags unintended public/cross-account access | Account/org-level |
| **IAM Policy Simulator** | Tests and validates IAM policies ŌĆö "will this policy allow Alice to do X?" | Interactive tool |

­¤ÆĪ **Use Access Advisor to implement least privilege:** find unused permissions ŌåÆ remove them from the policy.

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# Generate and download the credential report
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d > credential-report.csv

# Check last accessed info for a user (service-level)
aws iam generate-service-last-accessed-details --arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alice
aws iam get-service-last-accessed-details --job-id 

# List Access Analyzer findings
aws accessanalyzer list-findings --analyzer-arn 
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:**
* Credential Report: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_getting-report.html
* Access Analyzer: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-access-analyzer.html

questions.question: Which tool would you use to optimize user permissions by identifying which services he doesn't regularly (or at all) access?
questions.answer:
**IAM Access Advisor** ŌĆö it shows the "last accessed" information for each AWS service that a user, group, or role has permissions to access. 

By identifying services the user hasn't accessed in, say, 180 days, you can safely remove those permissions ŌĆö this is how you implement **least privilege over time**.

­¤ÆĪ The workflow: generate service last accessed details ŌåÆ review unused services ŌåÆ remove corresponding policy actions.

­¤ö¦ **CLI:**
```bash
# Generate a report for a specific user
aws iam generate-service-last-accessed-details \
  --arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alice

# Retrieve the report (use the JobId from the previous command)
aws iam get-service-last-accessed-details --job-id  \
  --query 'ServicesLastAccessed[?TotalAuthenticatedEntities==`0`].ServiceName'

# Find all users and their last activity
for user in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].UserName' --output text); do
  echo "=== $user ==="
  aws iam get-user --user-name "$user" --query 'User.PasswordLastUsed' --output text
done
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_access-advisor.html

questions.question: What type of IAM object would you use to allow inter-service communication?
questions.answer:
**IAM Role** ŌĆö Roles provide temporary credentials (via STS) that services use to call other AWS APIs. This is the standard pattern for inter-service communication in AWS.

Common patterns:
* EC2 ŌåÆ S3/DynamoDB/SQS
* Lambda ŌåÆ CloudWatch Logs + RDS
* ECS Task ŌåÆ Secrets Manager + S3
* CodeBuild ŌåÆ ECR + S3

­¤ÆĪ **Why not use IAM Users for services?** 
1. Users have long-lived access keys that need to be rotated manually
2. Keys are often hardcoded, creating a security risk
3. Roles auto-rotate credentials (STS tokens last max 36 hours)
4. Roles use instance metadata (EC2) or environment variables (Lambda) ŌĆö no keys to store

­¤ö¦ **CLI ŌĆö Demonstrate EC2 role usage:**
```bash
# Create a role for EC2 with S3 access
aws iam create-role \
  --role-name EC2S3Access \
  --assume-role-policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"Service":"ec2.amazonaws.com"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}'

aws iam attach-role-policy \
  --role-name EC2S3Access \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3FullAccess

# Create instance profile (needed for EC2 to use the role)
aws iam create-instance-profile --instance-profile-name EC2S3Profile
aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
  --instance-profile-name EC2S3Profile \
  --role-name EC2S3Access

# Launch EC2 with this instance profile
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --instance-type t3.micro \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=EC2S3Profile \
  --count 1
```

­¤ō¢ **Docs:** 
* IAM Roles: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html
* IAM Roles for EC2: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/iam-roles-for-amazon-ec2.html

questions.question: What is EC2?
questions.answer:
"a web service that provides secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud".
Read more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2)

questions.question: True or False? EC2 is a regional service
questions.answer: True. As opposed to IAM for example, which is a global service, EC2 is a regional service.

questions.question: What are some of the properties/configuration options of EC2 instances that can be set or modified?
questions.answer:
* OS (Linux, Windows)
* RAM and CPU
* Networking - IP, Card properties like speed
* Storage Space - (EBS, EFS, EC2 Instance Store)
* EC2 User Data
* Security groups

questions.question: What would you use for customizing EC2 instances? As in software installation, OS configuration, etc.
questions.answer: AMI. With AMI (Amazon Machine Image) you can customize EC2 instances by specifying which software to install, what OS changes should be applied, etc.

questions.question: What is AMI?
questions.answer:
Amazon Machine Images is "An Amazon Machine Image (AMI) provides the information required to launch an instance".
Read more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)

questions.question: What are the different sources for AMIs?
questions.answer:
* Personal AMIs - AMIs you create
* AWS Marketplace for AMIs - AMIs made by others, mostly sold for some price
* Public AMIs - Provided by AWS

questions.question: True or False? AMI are built for specific region
questions.answer: True (but they can be copied from one region to another).

questions.question: Describe in high-level the process of creating AMIs
questions.answer:
1. Start an EC2 instance
2. Customized the EC2 instance (install packages, change OS configuration, etc.)
3. Stop the instance (for avoiding data integrity issues)
4. Create EBS snapshot and build an AMI
5. To verify and test the AMI, launch an instance from the AMI

questions.question: What is an instance type?
questions.answer:
"the instance type that you specify determines the hardware of the host computer used for your instance"
Read more about instance types [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/instance-types.html)

questions.question: Explain the instance type naming convention
questions.answer:
Let's take for example the following instance type: m5.large

`m` is the instance class
`5` is the generation
`large` is the size of the instance (affects the spec properties like vCPUs and RAM)

questions.question: True or False? The following are instance types available for a user in AWS:    * Compute optimized   * Network optimized   * Web optimized
questions.answer: False. From the above list only compute optimized is available.

questions.question: Explain each of the following instance types:    * "Compute Optimized"   * "Memory Optimized"   * "Storage Optimized"
questions.answer:
Compute Optimized:

* Used for compute-intensive tasks
* It has high performance processors
* Use cases vary: gaming serves, machine learning, batch processing, etc.

Memory Optimized:

* Used for processing large data sets in memory
* Other use cases: high performance, databases, distributed cache stores

Storage Optimized:

* Used for storage intensive tasks - high read and write access to large data sets
* Use cases: databases, OLTP system, distributing file systems

questions.question: What can you attach to an EC2 instance in order to store data?
questions.answer: EBS

questions.question: Explain Amazon EBS
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AmazonEBS.html): "provides block level storage volumes for use with EC2 instances. EBS volumes behave like raw, unformatted block devices."

questions.question: What happens to EBS volumes when the instance is terminated?
questions.answer:
By default, the root volume is marked for deletion, while other volumes will still remain.
You can control what will happen to every volume upon termination.

questions.question: What happens to the EC2 disk (EBS) when the instance is stopped?
questions.answer: Disk is intact and can be used when the instance starts.

questions.question: True or False? EBS volumes are locked to a specific availability zone
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Explain EBS Snapshots
questions.answer: EBS snapshots used for making a backup of the EBS volume at point of time.

questions.question: What are the use cases for using EBS snapshots?
questions.answer:
* Backups of the data
* Moving the data between AZs

questions.question: Is it possible to attach the same EBS volume to multiple EC2 instances?
questions.answer: Yes, with multi-attach it's possible to attach a single EBS volume to multiple instances.

questions.question: True or False? EBS is a network drive hence, it requires network connectivity
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What EBS volume types are there?
questions.answer:
* HDD (st 1, sc 1): Low cost HDD volumes
* SSD
  * io1, io2: Highest performance SSD
  * gp2, gp3: General purpose SSD

questions.question: If you need an EBS volume for low latency workloads, which volume type would you use?
questions.answer: SSD - io1, io2

questions.question: If you need an EBS volume for workloads that require good performance but the cost is also an important aspect for you, which volume type would you use?
questions.answer: SSD - gp2, gp3

questions.question: If you need an EBS volume for high-throughput, which volume type would you use?
questions.answer: SSD - io1, io2

questions.question: If you need an EBS volume for infrequently data access, which volume type would you use?
questions.answer: HDD - sc1

questions.question: Which EBS volume types can be used as boot volumes for EC2 instances?
questions.answer: SSD: gp2, gp3, io1, io2

questions.question: True or False? In EBS gp2 volume type, IP will increase if the disk size increases
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: If you would like to have an hardware disk attached to your EC2 instead of a network one (EBS). What would you use?
questions.answer: EC2 Instance Store.

questions.question: Explain EC2 Instance Store. Why would someone choose to use it over other options?
questions.answer:
EC2 instance store provides better I/O performances when compared to EBS.
It is mostly used for cache and temporary data purposes.

questions.question: Are there any disadvantages in using instance store over EBS?
questions.answer: Yes, the data on instance store is lost when they are stopped.

questions.question: What is Amazon EFS?
questions.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/efs): "Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) provides a simple, scalable, fully managed elastic NFS file system for use with AWS Cloud services and on-premises resources."

In simpler words, it's a network file system you can mount on one or more EC2 instances.

questions.question: True or False? EFS is locked into a single availability zone
questions.answer: False. EFS can be mounted across multiple availability zones.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using EFS?
questions.answer:
* Data sharing (e.g. developers working on the same source control)
* Web serving
* Content management

questions.question: True or False? EFS only compatible with Linux based AMI
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? EFS requires the user to perform capacity planning as it doesn't scales automatically
questions.answer: False. EFS scales automatically and you pay-per-use.

questions.question: What EFS modes are there?
questions.answer:
* Performance mode
  * General purpose: used mainly for CMS, web serving, ... as it's optimal for latency sensitive applications
  * Max I/O: great for scaling to high levels of throughput and I/O operations per second
* Throughput mode
  * Bursting: scale throughput based on FS size
  * Provisioned: fixed throughput

questions.question: Which EFS mode would you use if you need to perform media processing?
questions.answer: Performance Mode (Max I/O): It provides high throughput and scales to operations per second. Mainly used for big data, media processing, etc.

questions.question: What is the default EFS mode?
questions.answer: Performance Mode (General Purpose): Used for web serving, CMS, ... anything that is sensitive to latency.

questions.question: What EFS storage tiers are there?
questions.answer:
* Standard: frequently accessed files
* Infrequent access: lower prices to store files but it also costs to retrieve them

questions.question: What EC2 pricing models are there?
questions.answer:
On Demand - pay a fixed rate by the hour/second with no commitment. You can provision and terminate it at any given time.
Reserved - you get capacity reservation, basically purchase an instance for a fixed time of period. The longer, the cheaper.
Spot - Enables you to bid whatever price you want for instances or pay the spot price.
Dedicated Hosts - physical EC2 server dedicated for your use.

questions.question: True or False? Reserved instance has to be used for a minimum of 1 year
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: Explain the following types of reserved instances:    * Convertible Reserved Instances   * Scheduled Reserved Instances
questions.answer:
* Convertible Reserved Instances: used for long running workloads but used when instance type might change during the period of time it's reserved
* Scheduled Reserved Instances: when you need to reserve an instance for a long period but you don't need it continuously (so for example you need it only in the morning)

questions.question: True or False? In EC2 On Demand, you pay per hour when using Linux or Windows and per second (after first minute) when using any other operating system
questions.answer: False. You pay per second (after the first minute) when using Windows or Linux and per hour for any other OS.

questions.question: You need an instance for short-term and the workload running on instance must not be interrupted. Which pricing model would you use?
questions.answer: On Demand is good for short-term non-interrupted workloads (but it also has the highest cost).

questions.question: You need an instance for running an application for a period of 2 years continuously, without changing instance type. Which pricing model would you use?
questions.answer: Reserved instances: they are cheaper than on-demand and the instance is yours for the chosen period of time.

questions.question: Which pricing model has potentially the biggest discount and what its advantage
questions.answer: Spot instances provide the biggest discount but has the disadvantage of risking losing them due bigger bid price.

questions.question: You need an instance for two years, but only between 10:00-15:00 every day. Which pricing model would you use?
questions.answer: Reserved instances from the "Scheduled Reserved Instances" type which allows you to reserve for specific time window (like 10:00-15:00 every day).

questions.question: You need an instance for running workloads. You don't care if they fail for a given moment as long as they run eventually. Which pricing model would you use?
questions.answer: Spot instances. The discount potential is the highest compared to all other pricing models. The disadvantage is that you can lose the instance at any point so, you must run only workloads that you are fine with them failing suddenly.

questions.question: You need a physical server only for your use. Which pricing model are you going to use?
questions.answer: EC2 Dedicated Host

questions.question: What are some of the differences between dedicated hosts and dedicated instances?
questions.answer:
In dedicated hosts you have per host billing, you have more visibility (sockets, cores, ...) and you can control where instance will be placed.
In dedicated instances the billing is per instance but you can't control placement and you don't have visibility of sockets, cores, ...

questions.question: For what use cases, EC2 dedicated hosts are useful for?
questions.answer:
* Compliance needs
* When the software license is complex (Bring Your Own License) and doesn't support cloud or multi-tenants
* Regulatory requirements

questions.question: What are Security Groups?
questions.answer:
"A security group acts as a virtual firewall that controls the traffic for one or more instances"
More on this subject [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-security-groups.html)

questions.question: True or False? Security groups only contain deny rules
questions.answer: False. Security groups only contain allow rules.

questions.question: True or False? One security group can be attached to multiple instances
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? Security groups are not locked down to a region and VPC (meaning you don't have to create a new one when switching regions)
questions.answer: False. They are locked down to regions and VPC.

questions.question: True or False? By default, when using security groups, all inbound traffic to an EC2 instance is blocked and all outbound traffic is allowed
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What is the advantage of referencing security groups from a given security group?
questions.answer:
Imagine you have an instance referencing two security groups, allowing to get inbound traffic from them.
Now imagine you have two instances, each using one of the security groups referenced in the instance we've just mentioned. This means you can get traffic from these two instances because they use security groups which referenced in the instance mentioned at the beginning. No need to use IPs.

questions.question: What can you attach to an EC2 instance in order to store data?
questions.answer: EBS

questions.question: What EC2 reserved instance types are there?
questions.answer:
Standard RI - most significant discount + suited for steady-state usage
Convertible RI - discount + change attribute of RI + suited for steady-state usage
Scheduled RI - launch within time windows you reserve

Learn more about EC2 RI [here](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/reserved-instances)

questions.question: For how long can reserved instances be reserved?
questions.answer: 1 or 3 years.

questions.question: What allows you to control inbound and outbound instance traffic?
questions.answer: Security Groups

questions.question: What bootstrapping means and how to use it in AWS EC2?
questions.answer:
Bootstrapping is about launching commands when a machine starts for the first time.
In AWS EC2 this is done using the EC2 user data script.

questions.question: You get time out when trying reach your application which runs on an EC2 instance. Specify one reason why it would possibly happen
questions.answer: Security group isn't configured properly.

questions.question: What is the AWS Instance Connect?
questions.answer: [AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Connect-using-EC2-Instance-Connect.html): "Amazon EC2 Instance Connect provides a simple and secure way to connect to your Linux instances using Secure Shell (SSH)."

questions.question: You try to run EC2 commands in an EC2 instance you've just created but it fails due to missing credentials. What would you do?
questions.answer:
DO NOT configure AWS credentials on the instance (this means anyone else in your account would be able to use and see your credentials).
The best practice is to attach an IAM role with sufficient permissions (like `IAMReadOnlyAccess`)

questions.question: True or False? Cancelling a Spot instance request terminates the instance
questions.answer:
False. When you cancel a Spot instance request, you are not terminating the instances created by it.
To terminate such instances, you must cancel the Spot instance request first.

questions.question: What are Spot Fleets?
questions.answer: Set of Spot instances and if you would like, also on-demand instances.

questions.question: What strategies are there to allocate Spot instances?
questions.answer:
* lowestPrice: launch instances from the pool that has the lowest price
* diversified: distributed across all pools
* capacityOptimized: optimized based on the number of instances

questions.question: From networking perspective, what do you get by default when running an EC2 instance?
questions.answer: A private IP and a public IP.

questions.question: Explain EC2 hibernate
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Hibernate.html: "Hibernation saves the contents from the instance memory (RAM) to your Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) root volume."

questions.question: True or False? Using EC2 hibernate option results in having faster instance boot
questions.answer: True. This is because the operating system isn't restarted or stopped.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using EC2 hibernate option?
questions.answer:
* Save RAM state
* Service with long time initialization
* Keep long-running processes

questions.question: What are some limitations of EC2 hibernate option?
questions.answer:
* Instance RAM size is limited
* Root volume must be encrypted EBS
* Hibernation time is limited
* Doesn't supports all instances types
* No support for bare metal. Only On-Demand and Reserved instances
* Doesn't supports all AMIs

questions.question: Explain what is EC2 Nitro
questions.answer:
* Next generation EC2 instances using new virtualization technology
* Better EBS: 64,000 EBS IOPS
* Better networking: HPC, IPv6
* Better security

questions.question: What CPU customization is available with EC2?
questions.answer:
* Modifying number of CPU cores (useful for high RAM and low CPU applications)
* Modifying number of threads per cure (useful for HPC workloads)

questions.question: Explain EC2 Capacity Reservations
questions.answer:
* Allows you to ensure you have EC2 capacity when you need it
* Usually combined with Reserved Instances and Saving Plans to achieve cost saving

questions.question: What is a launch template?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-launch-templates.html): "You can create a launch template that contains the configuration information to launch an instance. You can use launch templates to store launch parameters so that you do not have to specify them every time you launch an instance"

questions.question: What is the difference between Launch Configuration and Launch Template?
questions.answer:
Launch configuration is a legacy form of Launch Template that must be recreated every time you would like to update the configuration.

In addition, launch template has the clear benefits of:
  * Provision both On-Demand and Spot instances
  * supporting multiple versions
  * support creating parameters subsets (used for reuse and inheritance)

questions.question: Explain Elastic Network Interfaces (ENI)
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-eni.html): "An elastic network interface is a logical networking component in a VPC that represents a virtual network card."

questions.question: Name at least three attributes the Elastic Network Interfaces (ENI) can include
questions.answer:
1. One public IPv4 address
2. Mac Address
3. A primary private IPv4 address (from the address range of your VPC)

questions.question: True or False? ENI are not bound to a specific availability zone
questions.answer: False. ENI are bound to specific availability zone.

questions.question: True or False? ENI can be created independently of EC2 instances
questions.answer: True. They can be attached later on and on the fly (for failover purposes).

questions.question: What are "Placement Groups"?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/placement-groups.html): "When you launch a new EC2 instance, the EC2 service attempts to place the instance in such a way that all of your instances are spread out across underlying hardware to minimize correlated failures. You can use placement groups to influence the placement of a group of interdependent instances to meet the needs of your workload."

questions.question: What Placement Groups strategies are there?
questions.answer:
* Cluster: places instance close together in an AZ.
* Spread: spreads the instance across the hardware
* Partition: spreads the instances across different partitions (= different sets of hardware/racks) within an AZ

questions.question: For each of the following scenarios choose a placement group strategy:    * High availability is top priority   * Low latency between instances   * Instances must be isolated from each other   * Big Data applications that are partition aware   * Big Data process that needs to end quickly
questions.answer:
* High availability is top priority - Spread
  * Low latency between instances - Cluster
  * Instances must be isolated from each other - Spread
  * Big Data applications that are partition aware - Partition
  * Big Data process that needs to end quickly - Cluster

questions.question: What are the cons and pros of the "Cluster" placement group strategy?
questions.answer:
Cons: if the hardware fails, all instances fail
Pros: Low latency & high throughput network

questions.question: What are the cons and pros of the "Spread" placement group strategy?
questions.answer:
Cons:
  * Current limitation is 7 instances per AZ (per replacement group)
Pros:
  * Maximized high availability (instances on different hardware, span across AZs)

questions.question: What is VPC?
questions.answer:
"A logically isolated section of the AWS cloud where you can launch AWS resources in a virtual network that you define"
Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc).

questions.question: True or False? VPC spans multiple regions
questions.answer: False

questions.question: True or False? It's possible to have multiple VPCs in one region
questions.answer: True. As of today, the soft limit is 5.

questions.question: True or False? Subnets belong to the same VPC, can be in different availability zones
questions.answer: True. Just to clarify, a single subnet resides entirely in one AZ.

questions.question: You have noticed your VPC's subnets (which use x.x.x.x/20 CIDR) have 4096 available IP addresses although this CIDR should have 4096 addresses. What is the reason for that?
questions.answer: AWS reserves 5 IP addresses in each subnet - first 4 and the last one, and so they aren't available for use.

questions.question: What AWS uses the 5 reserved IP addresses for?
questions.answer:
x.x.x.0 - network address
x.x.x.1 - VPC router
x.x.x.2 - DNS mapping
x.x.x.3 - future use
x.x.x.255 - broadcast address

questions.question: What is an Internet Gateway?
questions.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Internet_Gateway.html): "component that allows communication between instances in your VPC and the internet"

In addition it's good to know that IGW is:
  * Highly available and redundant
  * Not porivding internet access by its own (you need route tables to be edited)
  * Created separately from VPC

questions.question: True or False? One or more VPCs can be attached to one Internet Gateway
questions.answer: False. Only one VPC can be attached to one IGW and vice versa

questions.question: True or False? NACL allow or deny traffic on the subnet level
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What is VPC peering?
questions.answer: [docs.aws](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/what-is-vpc-peering.html): "A VPC peering connection is a networking connection between two VPCs that enables you to route traffic between them using private IPv4 addresses or IPv6 addresses."

questions.question: True or False? Multiple Internet Gateways can be attached to one VPC
questions.answer: False. Only one internet gateway can be attached to a single VPC.

questions.question: You've restarted your EC2 instance and the public IP has changed. How would you deal with it so it won't happen?
questions.answer: Use Elastic IP which provides you a fixed IP address.

questions.question: When creating a new VPC, there is an option called "Tenancy". What is it used for?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/create-vpc.html): `Tenancy` option defines if EC2 instances that you launch into the VPC will run on hardware that's shared with other AWS accounts or on hardware that's dedicated for your use only.

questions.question: What is an Elastic IP address?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/elastic-ip-addresses-eip.html): "An Elastic IP address is a static IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing. An Elastic IP address is allocated to your AWS account, and is yours until you release it. By using an Elastic IP address, you can mask the failure of an instance or software by rapidly remapping the address to another instance in your account."

questions.question: Why would you use an Elastic IP address?
questions.answer: Let's say you have an instance that you need to shutdown or perform some maintenance on. In that case, what you would want to do is to move the Elastic IP address to another instance that is operational, until you finish to perform the maintenance and then you can move it back to the original instance (or keep it assigned to the second one).

questions.question: True or False? When stopping and starting an EC2 instance, its public IP changes
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What are the best practices around Elastic IP?
questions.answer: The best practice is actually not using them in the first place. It's more common to use a load balancer without a public IP or use a random public IP and register a DNS record to it

questions.question: True or False? An Elastic IP is free, as long it's not associated with an EC2 instance
questions.answer: False. An Elastic IP is free of charge as long as **it is ** associated with an EC2 instance. This instance should be running and should have only one Elastic IP.

questions.question: True or False? Route Tables used to allow or deny traffic from the internet to AWS instances
questions.answer: False.

questions.question: Explain Security Groups and Network ACLs
questions.answer:
* NACL - security layer on the subnet level.
* Security Group - security layer on the instance level.

Read more about it [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-security-groups.html) and [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_SecurityGroups.html)

questions.question: What is AWS Direct Connect?
questions.answer: Allows you to connect your corporate network to AWS network.

questions.question: What would you use if you need a fixed public IP for your EC2 instance?
questions.answer: Elastic IP

questions.question: Kratos, your colleague, decided to use a subnet of /27 because he needs 29 IP addresses for EC2 instances. Is Kratos right?
questions.answer:
No. Since AWS reserves 5 IP addresses for every subnet, Kratos will have 32-5=27 addresses and this is less than what he needs (29).

It's better if Kratos uses a subnet of size /26 but good luck telling him that.

questions.question: True or False? By default, any new account has a default VPC
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: True or False? Default VPC doesn't have internet connectivity and any launched EC2 will only have a private IP assigned
questions.answer:
False. The default VPC has internet connectivity and any launched EC2 instance gets a public IPv4 address.

In addition, any launched EC2 instance gets a public and private DNS names.

questions.question: Which of the following is included with default VPC?  * Internet gateway connected to the default VPC * A route to main route table that points all traffic to internet gateway * Default public subnet * Default /16 IPv4 CIDR block
questions.answer: All of them :)

questions.question: Explain what is AWS Lambda
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Lambda lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers. You pay only for the compute time you consume."

Read more on it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda)

questions.question: True or False? In AWS Lambda, you are charged as long as a function exists, regardless of whether it's running or not
questions.answer: False. Charges are being made when the function is executed for the time it takes to execute and compute resources it uses.

questions.question: Which of the following set of languages Lambda supports?  - R, Swift, Rust, Kotlin - Python, Ruby, Go, Kotlin, Bash - Python, Ruby, PHP, PowerShell, C#, Perl - Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, Groovy, C++ - Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, PowerShell, C#
questions.answer: - Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, PowerShell, C#

questions.question: True or False? Basic lambda permissions allow you only to upload logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What's one of the issues with the current architecture?
questions.answer:
Users shouldn't access directly AWS Lambda directly. If you'd to like to expose your Lambda function to users a better approach would be to set up API Gateway endpoint between the users and the Lambda function.

This not only provides enhanced security but also easier access for the user where he can use HTTP or HTTPS for accessing the function.

questions.question: Specify one or more use cases for using AWS Lambda
questions.answer:
- Uploading images to S3 and tagging them or inserting information on the images to a database
- Uploading videos to S3 and edit them or add subtitles/captions to them and store the result in S3
- Use SNS and/or SQS to trigger functions based on notifications or messages received from these services.
- Cron Jobs: Use Lambda together with CloudWatch events to schedule tasks/functions periodically.

questions.question: You run an architecture where you have a Lambda function that uploads images to S3 bucket and stores information on the images in DynamoDB. You would like to expose the function to users so they can invoke it. Your friend Carlos suggests you expose the credentials to the Lambda function. What's your take on that?
questions.answer:
That's a big no. You shouldn't let users direct access to your Lambda function.

The way to go here and expose the Lambda function to users is to to an API Gateway endpoint.

questions.question: What is Amazon ECS?
questions.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/ecs): "Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) is a fully managed container orchestration service. Customers such as Duolingo, Samsung, GE, and Cook Pad use ECS to run their most sensitive and mission critical applications because of its security, reliability, and scalability."

In simpler words, it allows you to launch containers on AWS.
While AWS takes care of starting/stopping containers, you need to provision and maintain the infrastructure where the containers are running (EC2 instances).

questions.question: What one should do in order to make EC2 instance part of an ECS cluster?
questions.answer: Install ECS agent on it. Some AMIs have built-in configuration for that.

questions.question: What ECS launch types are there?
questions.answer:
* EC2 Instance
* AWS Fargate

questions.question: What is Amazon ECR?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/ecr): "Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a fully-managed Docker container registry that makes it easy for developers to store, manage, and deploy Docker container images."

questions.question: What the role "EC2 Instance Profile" is used for in regards to ECS?
questions.answer:
EC2 Instance Profile used by ECS agent on an EC2 instance to:

  * Make API calls to ECS Service
  * Send logs to CloudWatch from the container
  * Use secrets defined in SSM Parameter Store or Secrets Manager
  * Pull container images from ECR (Registry)

questions.question: How to share data between containers (some from ECS and some from Fargate)?
questions.answer: Using EFS is a good way to share data between containers and it works also between different AZs.

questions.question: What is AWS Fargate?
questions.answer:
[Amazon Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/fargate): "AWS Fargate is a serverless, pay-as-you-go compute engine that lets you focus on building applications without managing servers. AWS Fargate is compatible with both Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) and Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)"

In simpler words, AWS Fargate allows you launch containers on AWS without worrying about managing infrastructure. It runs containers based on the CPU and RAM you need.

questions.question: How AWS Fargate different from AWS ECS?
questions.answer:
In AWS ECS, you manage the infrastructure - you need to provision and configure the EC2 instances.
While in AWS Fargate, you don't provision or manage the infrastructure, you simply focus on launching Docker containers. You can think of it as the serverless version of AWS ECS.

questions.question: True or False? Fargate creates an ENI for every task it runs
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: Explain what is AWS S3?
questions.answer:
- S3 is a object storage service which is fast, scalable and durable. S3 enables customers to upload, download or store any file or object that is up to 5 TB in size.
- S3 stands for: Simple Storage Service
- As a user you don't have to worry about filesystems or disk space

questions.question: What is a bucket?
questions.answer: An S3 bucket is a resource which is similar to folders in a file system and allows storing objects, which consist of data.

questions.question: True or False? Buckets are defined globally
questions.answer: False. They are defined at the region level.

questions.question: True or False? A bucket name must be globally unique
questions.answer: True

questions.question: How to rename a bucket in S3?
questions.answer:
A S3 bucket name is immutable. That means it's not possible to change it, without removing and creating a new bucket.

This is why the process for renaming a bucket is as follows:

* Create a new bucket with the desired name
* Move the data from the old bucket to it
* Delete the old bucket

With the AWS CLI that would be:

```sh
# Create new bucket
aws s3 mb s3://[NEW_BUCKET_NAME]
# Sync the content from the old bucket to the new bucket
$ aws s3 sync s3://[OLD_BUCKET_NAME] s3://[NEW_BUCKET_NAME]
# Remove old bucket
$ aws s3 rb --force s3://[OLD_BUCKET_NAME]
```

questions.question: True or False? The max object size a user can upload in one go, is 5TB
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Explain "Multi-part upload"
questions.answer: [Amazon docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/mpuoverview.html): "Multipart upload allows you to upload a single object as a set of parts. Each part is a contiguous portion of the object's data...In general, when your object size reaches 100 MB, you should consider using multipart uploads instead of uploading the object in a single operation."

questions.question: Explain "Object Versioning"
questions.answer: When enabled at a bucket level, versioning allows you to upload new version of files, overriding previous version and so be able to easily roll-back and protect your data from being permanently deleted.

questions.question: Explain the following:    - Object Lifecycles   - Object Sharing
questions.answer:
* Object Lifecycles - Transfer objects between storage classes based on defined rules of time periods
  * Object Sharing - Share objects via a URL link

questions.question: Explain Object Durability and Object Availability
questions.answer:
Object Durability: The percent over a one-year time period that a file will not be lost
Object Availability: The percent over a one-year time period that a file will be accessible

questions.question: True or False? Every new S3 bucket is public by default
questions.answer: False. A newly created bucket is private unless it was configured to be public.

questions.question: What's a presigned URL?
questions.answer:
Since every newly created bucket is by default private it doesn't allows to share files with users. Even if the person who uploaded them tries to view them, it gets denied.

A presigned URL is a way to bypass that and allow sharing the files with users by including the credentials (token) as part of the URL. It can be done for limited time.

questions.question: What security measures have you taken in context of S3?
questions.answer:
* Don't make a bucket public.
	* Enable encryption if it's disabled.
    * Define an access policy

questions.question: What encryption types supported by S3?
questions.answer:
* SSE-S3
* SSE-KMS
* SSE-C

questions.question: Describe shortly how SSE-S3 (AES) encryption works
questions.answer:
1. You upload a file to S3 using HTTP (or HTTPS) and header
2. S3 uses the managed data key to encrypt it
3. S3 stores the encrypted object in the bucket

questions.question: True or False? In case of SSE-S3 (AES-256) encryption, you manage the key
questions.answer: False. S3 manages the key and uses AES-256 algorithm for the encryption.

questions.question: Who or what manages the keys in the case of SSE-KMS encryption?
questions.answer: The KMS service.

questions.question: Why would someone choose to use SSE-KMS instead of SSE-S3?
questions.answer: SS3-KMS provides control over who has access to the keys and you can also enabled audit trail.

questions.question: True or False? In case of SSE-C encryption, both S3 and you manage the keys
questions.answer: False. You manage the keys. It's customer provided keys.

questions.question: True or False? In case of SSE-C HTTPS must be used and encryption key must be provided in headers for every HTTP request
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: Describe shortly how SSE-C encryption works
questions.answer:
1. User uploads a file to S3 using HTTPS while providing data key in the header
2. AWS S3 performs the encryption using the provided data key and encrypted object is stored in the bucket

If a user would like to get the object, the same data key would have to be provided.

questions.question: With which string an header starts?  * x-zmz * x-amz * x-ama
questions.answer: x-amz

questions.question: What is a storage class? What storage classes are there?
questions.answer:
Each object has a storage class assigned to, affecting its availability and durability. This also has effect on costs.
Storage classes offered today:
  * Standard:
    * Used for general, all-purpose storage (mostly storage that needs to be accessed frequently)
    * The most expensive storage class
    * 11x9% durability
    * 2x9% availability
    * Default storage class

  * Standard-IA (Infrequent Access)
    * Long lived, infrequently accessed data but must be available the moment it's being accessed
    * 11x9% durability
    * 99.90% availability

  * One Zone-IA (Infrequent Access):
    * Long-lived, infrequently accessed, non-critical data
    * Less expensive than Standard and Standard-IA storage classes
    * 2x9% durability
    * 99.50% availability

  * Intelligent-Tiering:
    * Long-lived data with changing or unknown access patterns. Basically, In this class the data automatically moves to the class most suitable for you based on usage patterns
    * Price depends on the used class
    * 11x9% durability
    * 99.90% availability

  * Glacier: Archive data with retrieval time ranging from minutes to hours
  * Glacier Deep Archive: Archive data that rarely, if ever, needs to be accessed with retrieval times in hours
  * Both Glacier and Glacier Deep Archive are:
    * The most cheap storage classes
    * have 9x9% durability

More on storage classes [here](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/storage-classes)

questions.question: A customer would like to move data which is rarely accessed from standard storage class to the most cheapest class there is. Which storage class should be used?    * One Zone-IA   * Glacier Deep Archive   * Intelligent-Tiering
questions.answer: Glacier Deep Archive

questions.question: What Glacier retrieval options are available for the user?
questions.answer: Expedited, Standard and Bulk

questions.question: True or False? Each AWS account can store up to 500 PetaByte of data. Any additional storage will cost double
questions.answer: False. Unlimited capacity.

questions.question: Explain what is Storage Gateway
questions.answer:
"AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid cloud storage service that gives you on-premises access to virtually unlimited cloud storage".
More on Storage Gateway [here](https://aws.amazon.com/storagegateway)

questions.question: Explain the following Storage Gateway deployments types    * File Gateway   * Volume Gateway   * Tape Gateway
questions.answer: Explained in detail [here](https://aws.amazon.com/storagegateway/faqs)

questions.question: What is the difference between stored volumes and cached volumes?
questions.answer:
Stored Volumes - Data is located at customer's data center and periodically backed up to AWS
Cached Volumes - Data is stored in AWS cloud and cached at customer's data center for quick access

questions.question: What is "Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration"?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration enables fast, easy, and secure transfers of files over long distances between your client and an S3 bucket"

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/transfer-acceleration.html)

questions.question: Explain data consistency
questions.answer: S3 Data Consistency provides strong read-after-write consistency for PUT and DELETE requests of objects in the S3 bucket in all AWS Regions. S3 always return latest file version.

questions.question: Can you host dynamic websites on S3? What about static websites?
questions.answer: No. S3 support only statis hosts. On a static website, individual webpages include static content. They might also contain client-side scripts. By contrast, a dynamic website relies on server-side processing, including server-side scripts such as PHP, JSP, or ASP.NET. Amazon S3 does not support server-side scripting.

questions.question: In regards to disaster recovery, what is RTO and RPO?
questions.answer:
RTO - The maximum acceptable length of time that your application can be offline.

RPO - The maximum acceptable length of time during which data might be lost from your application due to an incident.

questions.question: What types of disaster recovery techniques AWS supports?
questions.answer:
* The Cold Method - Periodically backups and sending the backups off-site
* Pilot Light - Data is mirrored to an environment which is always running
* Warm Standby - Running scaled down version of production environment
* Multi-site - Duplicated environment that is always running

questions.question: Which disaster recovery option has the highest downtime and which has the lowest?
questions.answer:
Lowest - Multi-site
Highest - The cold method

questions.question: Explain what is CloudFront
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon CloudFront is a fast content delivery network (CDN) service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency, high transfer speeds, all within a developer-friendly environment."

More on CloudFront [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront)

questions.question: True or False?. Objects are cached for the life of TTL
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What is AWS Snowball?
questions.answer: A transport solution which was designed for transferring large amounts of data (petabyte-scale) into and out the AWS cloud.

questions.question: How can a company ensure their web application continues to operate if it becomes unavailable in its current single region?
questions.answer: Deploy the application in multiple Regions. Use Amazon Route 53 DNS health checks to route traffic to a healthy Region

questions.question: What is ELB (Elastic Load Balancing)?
questions.answer: [AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing): "Elastic Load Balancing automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets, such as Amazon EC2 instances, containers, IP addresses, and Lambda functions."

questions.question: True or False? Elastic Load Balancer is a managed resource (= AWS takes care of it)
questions.answer: True. AWS responsible for making sure ELB is operational and takes care of lifecycle operations like upgrades, maintenance and high availability.

questions.question: What types of AWS load balancers are there?
questions.answer:
* Classic Load Balancer (CLB): Mainly for TCP (layer 4) and HTTP, HTTPS (layer 7)
* Application Load Balancer (ALB): Mainly for HTTP, HTTPS and WebSocket
* Network Load Balancer (NLB): Mainly for TCP, TLS and UDP
* Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB): Mainly for layer 3 operations (IP protocol)

questions.question: Which load balancer would you use for services which use HTTP or HTTPS traffic?
questions.answer: Application Load Balancer (ALB).

questions.question: What are some use cases for using Gateway Load Balancer?
questions.answer:
* Intrusion Detection
* Firewall
* Payload manipulation

questions.question: Explain "health checks" in the context of AWS ELB
questions.answer:
Health checks used by ELB to check whether EC2 instance(s) are properly working.
If health checks fail, ELB knows to not forward traffic to that specific EC2 instance where the health checks failed.

questions.question: True or False? AWS ELB health checks are done on a port and a route
questions.answer:
True.

For example, port `2017` and endpoint `/health`.

questions.question: What types of load balancers are supported in EC2 and what are they used for?
questions.answer:
* Application LB - layer 7 traffic
* Network LB - ultra-high performances or static IP address (layer 4)
* Classic LB - low costs, good for test or dev environments (retired by August 15, 2022)
* Gateway LB - transparent network gateway and and distributes traffic such as firewalls, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and deep packet inspection systems. (layer 3)

questions.question: Which type of AWS load balancer is used in the following drawing?
questions.answer: Application Load Balancer (routing based on different endpoints + HTTP is used).

questions.question: What are possible target groups for ALB (Application Load Balancer)?
questions.answer:
* EC2 tasks
* ECS instances
* Lambda functions
* Private IP Addresses

questions.question: True or False? ALB can route only to a single route group
questions.answer: False. ALB can route to multiple target groups.

questions.question: If you wanted to analyze network traffic, you would use the `____ load balancer`
questions.answer: Gateway Load Balancer

questions.question: Who has better latency? Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer?
questions.answer: Network Load Balancer (~100 ms) as ALB has a latency of ~400 ms

questions.question: True or False? Network load balancer has one static IP per availability zone
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: What are the supported target groups for network load balancer?
questions.answer:
* EC2 instance
* IP addresses
* Application Load Balancer

questions.question: What are the supported target groups for gateway load balancer?
questions.answer:
* EC2 instance
* IP addresses (must be private IPs)

questions.question: Name one use case for using application load balancer as a target group for network load balancer
questions.answer: You might want to have a fixed IP address (NLB) and then forward HTTP traffic based on path, query, ... which is then done by ALB

questions.question: What are some use cases for using Network Load Balancer?
questions.answer:
* TCP, UDP traffic
* Extreme performance

questions.question: True or False? Network load balancers operate in layer 4
questions.answer: True. They forward TCP, UDP traffic.

questions.question: True or False? It's possible to enable sticky session for network load balancer so the same client is always redirected to the same instance
questions.answer: False. This is only supported in Classic Load Balancer and Application Load Balancer.

questions.question: Explain Cross Zone Load Balancing
questions.answer: With cross zone load balancing, traffic distributed evenly across all (registered) instances in all the availability zones.

questions.question: True or False? For network load balancer, cross zone load balancing is always on and can't be disabled
questions.answer: False. It's disabled by default

questions.question: True or False? In regards to cross zone load balancing, AWS charges you for inter AZ data in network load balancer but no in application load balancer
questions.answer: True. It charges for inter AZ data in network load balancer, but not in application load balancer

questions.question: True or False? Both ALB and NLB support multiple listeners with multiple SSL certificates
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Explain Deregistration Delay (or Connection Draining) in regards to ELB
questions.answer: The period of time or process of "draining" instances from requests/traffic (basically let it complete all active connections but don't start new ones) so it can be de-registered eventually and ELB won't send requests/traffic to it anymore.

questions.question: At what network level/layer a Network Load Balancer operates?
questions.answer: Layer 4

questions.question: True or False? With ALB (Application Load Balancer) it's possible to do routing based on query string and/or headers
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: True or False? For application load balancer, cross zone load balancing is always on and can't be disabled
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Explain Auto Scaling Group
questions.answer: [Amazon Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/AutoScalingGroup.html): "An Auto Scaling group contains a collection of Amazon EC2 instances that are treated as a logical grouping for the purposes of automatic scaling and management. An Auto Scaling group also enables you to use Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling features such as health check replacements and scaling policies"

questions.question: You have two instance running as part of ASG. You change the desired capacity to 1. What will be the outcome of this change?
questions.answer: One of the instances will be terminated.

questions.question: How can you customize the trigger for the scaling in/out of an auto scaling group?
questions.answer: One way is to use CloudWatch alarms where an alarm will monitor a metric and based on a certain value (or range) you can choose to scale-in or scale-out the ASG.

questions.question: What are some metrics/rules used for auto scaling
questions.answer:
* Network In/Out
* Number of requests on ELB per instance
* Average CPU, RAM usage

questions.question: What is dynamic Scaling policy in regards to Auto Scaling Groups?
questions.answer:
A policy in which scaling will occur automatically based on different metrics.

There are 3 types:

1. Target Tracking Scaling: scale when the baseline changes (e.g. CPU is over 60%)
2. Step Scaling: more granular scaling where you can choose different actions for different metrics values (e.g. when CPU less than 20%, remove one instance. When CPU is over 40%, add 3 instances)
3. Scheduled Actions: set in advance scaling for specific period of time (e.g. add instances on Monday between 10:00 am to 11:00 am)

questions.question: What is a predictive scaling policy in regards to Auto Scaling Groups?
questions.answer: Scale by analyzing historical load and schedule scaling based on forecast load.

questions.question: Explain scaling cooldowns in regards to Auto Scaling Groups
questions.answer: During a scaling cooldown, ASG will not terminate or launch additional instances. The cooldown happens after scaling activity and the reason for this behaviour is that some metrics have to be collected and stabilize before another scaling operating can take place.

questions.question: Explain the default ASG termination policy
questions.answer:
1. It finds the AZ which the most number of EC2 instances
2. If number of instances > 1, choose the one with oldest launch configuration, template and terminate it
