questions.question: True or False? by default, ASG tries to balance the number of instances across AZ
questions.answer: True, this is why when it terminates instances, it chooses the AZ with the most instances.

questions.question: Explain Lifecycle hooks in regards to Auto Scaling Groups
questions.answer: Lifecycle hooks allows you perform extra steps before the instance goes in service (During pending state) or before it terminates (during terminating state).

questions.question: If you use ASG and you would like to run extra steps before the instance goes in service, what will you use?
questions.answer: Lifecycle hooks in pending state.

questions.question: Describe one way to test ASG actually works
questions.answer:
In Linux instances, you can install the 'stress' package and run stress to load the system for certain period of time and see if ASG kicks in by adding additional capacity (= more instances).

For example: `sudo stress --cpu 100 --timeout 20`

questions.question: What is the shared responsibility model? What AWS is responsible for and what the user is responsible for based on the shared responsibility model?
questions.answer:
The shared responsibility model defines what the customer is responsible for and what AWS is responsible for.

More on the shared responsibility model [here](https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model)

questions.question: True or False? Based on the shared responsibility model, Amazon is responsible for physical CPUs and security groups on instances
questions.answer: False. It is responsible for Hardware in its sites but not for security groups which created and managed by the users.

questions.question: Explain "Shared Controls" in regards to the shared responsibility model
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "apply to both the infrastructure layer and customer layers, but in completely separate contexts or perspectives. In a shared control, AWS provides the requirements for the infrastructure and the customer must provide their own control implementation within their use of AWS services"

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model)

questions.question: How to secure instances in AWS?
questions.answer:
* Instance IAM roles should have minimal permissions needed. You don't want an instance-level incident to become an account-level incident
  * Use "AWS System Manager Session Manager" for SSH
  * Using latest OS images with your instances

questions.question: What is AWS Artifact?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Artifact is your go-to, central resource for compliance-related information that matters to you. It provides on-demand access to AWSŌĆÖ security and compliance reports and select online agreements."

Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/artifact)

questions.question: What is AWS Inspector?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon Inspector is an automated security assessment service that helps improve the security and compliance of applications deployed on AWS. Amazon Inspector automatically assesses applications for exposure, vulnerabilities, and deviations from best practices.""

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/inspector)

questions.question: What is AWS Guarduty?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior to protect your Amazon Web Services accounts, workloads, and data stored in Amazon S3" 
Monitor VPC Flow lows, DNS logs, CloudTrail S3 events and CloudTrail Mgmt events.

questions.question: What is AWS Shield?
questions.answer: AWS definition: "AWS Shield is a managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection service that safeguards applications running on AWS."

questions.question: What is AWS CloudHSM?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS CloudHSM is a cloud-based hardware security module (HSM) that enables you to easily generate and use your own encryption keys on the AWS Cloud."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudhsm)

questions.question: True or False? AWS Inspector can perform both network and host assessments
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What is AWS Key Management Service (KMS)?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "KMS makes it easy for you to create and manage cryptographic keys and control their use across a wide range of AWS services and in your applications."
More on KMS [here](https://aws.amazon.com/kms)

questions.question: What is AWS Acceptable Use Policy?
questions.answer:
It describes prohibited uses of the web services offered by AWS.
More on AWS Acceptable Use Policy [here](https://aws.amazon.com/aup)

questions.question: True or False? A user is not allowed to perform penetration testing on any of the AWS services
questions.answer: False. On some services, like EC2, CloudFront and RDS, penetration testing is allowed.

questions.question: True or False? DDoS attack is an example of allowed penetration testing activity
questions.answer: False.

questions.question: True or False? AWS Access Key is a type of MFA device used for AWS resources protection
questions.answer: False. Security key is an example of an MFA device.

questions.question: What is Amazon Cognito?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Cognito handles user authentication and authorization for your web and mobile apps."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/index.html)

questions.question: What is AWS ACM?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Certificate Manager is a service that lets you easily provision, manage, and deploy public and private Secure Sockets Layer/Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) certificates for use with AWS services and your internal connected resources."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/certificate-manager)

questions.question: What is AWS RDS?
questions.answer:
* Relational Database Service
* Managed DB service (you can't ssh the machine)
* Supports multiple DBs: MySQL, Oracle, Aurora (AWS Proprietary), ...

questions.question: Why to use AWS RDS instead of launching an EC2 instance and install a database on it?
questions.answer:
AWS RDS is a managed service, that means it's automatically provisioned and patched for you.

In addition, it provides you with continuous backup (and the ability to restore from any point of time), scaling capability (both horizontal and vertical), monitoring dashboard and read replicas.

questions.question: What do you know about RDS backups?
questions.answer:
* Automated backups
* Full daily backup (done during maintenance window)
* Transactions logs backup every 5 minutes
* Retention can be increased and by default it's 7 days

questions.question: Explain AWS RDS Storage Auto Scaling
questions.answer:
* RDS storage can automatically be increased upon lack in storage
* The user needs to set "Maximum Storage Threshold" to have some limit on storage scaling
* Use cases: applications with unpredictable workloads
* Supports multiple RDS database engines

questions.question: Explain Amazon RDS Read Replicas
questions.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/rds/features/read-replicas): "Amazon RDS Read Replicas provide enhanced performance and durability for RDS database (DB) instances. They make it easy to elastically scale out beyond the capacity constraints of a single DB instance for read-heavy database workloads."

In simpler words, it allows you to scale your reads.

questions.question: True or False? RDS read replicas are supported within az, cross az and cross region
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? RDS read replicas are asynchronous
questions.answer: True. This is done so the reads are consistent.

questions.question: True or False? Amazon RDS supports MongoDB
questions.answer: False. RDS is relational database and MongoDB is a NoSQL db.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using RDS read replicas?
questions.answer: You have a main application which works against your database but you would like to add additional app, one used for logging, analytics, ... so you prefer it won't use the same database. In this case, you create a read replica instance and the second application works against that instance.

questions.question: Explain RDS Multi Availability Zone
questions.answer:
* RDS multi AZ used mainly for disaster recovery purposes
* There is an RDS master instance and in another AZ an RDS standby instance
* The data is synced synchronously between them
* The user, application is accessing one DNS name and where there is a failure with the master instance, the DNS name moves to the standby instance, so the failover done automatically

questions.question: True or False? Moving AWS RDS from single AZ to multi AZ is an operation with downtime (meaning there is a need to stop the DB)
questions.answer: False. It's a zero downtime operation = no need to stop the database.

questions.question: How AWS RDS switches from single AZ to multi AZ?
questions.answer:
1. Snapshot is taken by RDS
2. The snapshot is restored to another, standby, RDS instance
3. Synchronization is enabled between the two instances

questions.question: True or False? RDS encryption should be defined at launch time
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? in regards to RDS, replicas can be encrypted even if the master isn't encrypted
questions.answer: False

questions.question: How to make RDS snapshots encrypted?
questions.answer:
* If RDS database is encrypted then, the snapshot itself is also encrypted
* If RDS database isn't encrypted then, the snapshot itself isn't encrypted and then you can copy the un-encrypted snapshot to created an encrypted copy

questions.question: How to encrypt an un-encrypted RDS instance?
questions.answer: Create a copy of the un-encrypted instance -> copy the snapshot to create an encrypted copy -> restore the database from the encrypted snapshot -> migrate the application to work against the copied instance -> remove the original DB instance

questions.question: How IAM authentication works with RDS?
questions.answer:
For example:

1. EC2 instance uses IAM role to make an API call to get auth token
2. The token, with SSL encryption, is used for accessing the RDS instance

Note: The token has a lifetime of 15 minutes

questions.question: True or False? In case of RDS (not Aurora), read replicas require you to change the SQL connection string
questions.answer: True. Since read replicas add endpoints, each with its own DNS name, you need to modify your app to reference these new endpoints to balance the load read.

questions.question: What do you know about Amazon Aurora?
questions.answer:
* A MySQL & Postgresql based relational database.
* Proprietary technology from AWS
* The default database proposed for the user when using RDS for creating a database.
* Storage automatically grows in increments of 10 GiB
* HA native - failover in instant
* Has better performances over MySQL and Postgres
* Supports 15 replicas (while MySQL supports 5)

questions.question: True or False? Aurora stores 4 copies of your data across 2 availability zones
questions.answer: False. It stores 6 copies across 3 availability zones

questions.question: True or False? Aurora support self healing where corrupted data replaced by doing peer-to-peer replication
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? Aurora storage is striped across 20 volumes
questions.answer: False. 100 volumes.

questions.question: True or False? It's possible to scale Aurora replicas
questions.answer: True. If your read replica instances exhaust their CPU, you can scale by adding more instances

questions.question: Explain Aurora Serverless. What use cases is it good for?
questions.answer:
* Aurora serverless is an automated database instantiation and it's auto scaled based on an actual usage
* It's good mainly for infrequent or unpredictable workflows
* You pay per second so it can eventually be more cost effective

questions.question: What is the use case for Aurora multi-master?
questions.answer: Aurora multi-master is perfect for a use case where you want to have instant failover for write node.

questions.question: Explain "Point-in-Time Recovery" feature in DynamoDB
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "You can create on-demand backups of your Amazon DynamoDB tables, or you can enable continuous backups using point-in-time recovery. For more information about on-demand backups, see On-Demand Backup and Restore for DynamoDB."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/PointInTimeRecovery.html)

questions.question: Explain "Global Tables" in DynamoDB
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "A global table is a collection of one or more replica tables, all owned by a single AWS account."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/V2globaltables_HowItWorks.html)

questions.question: What is DynamoDB Accelerator?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a fully managed, highly available, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers up to a 10x performance improvement ŌĆō from milliseconds to microseconds..."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/dax)

questions.question: What is AWS ElastiCache? In what use case should it be used?
questions.answer:
Amazon Elasticache is a fully managed Redis or Memcached in-memory data store.
It's great for read-intensive workloads where the common data/queries are cached and apps/users access the cache instead of the primary database.

questions.question: Describe the workflow of an application using the cache in AWS
questions.answer:
1. The application performs a query against the DB. There is a check to see if the data is in the cache
  1. If it is, it's a "cache hit" and the data is retrieved from there
  2. If it's not in there, it's a "cache miss" and the data is pulled from the database
   1. The data is then also written to the cache (assuming it is often accessed) and next time the user queries for the same data, it might be retrieved from the cache (depends on how much time passed and whether this specific data was invalidated or not)

questions.question: How can you make an application stateless using ElastiCache?
questions.answer:
Let's say you have multiple instances running the same application and every time you use the application, it creates a user session.
This user session can be stored in ElastiCache so even if the user contacts a different instance of the application, the application can retrieve the session from the ElsatiCache.

questions.question: You need a highly available cache with backup and restore features. Which one would you use?
questions.answer: ElastiCache Redis.

questions.question: You need a cache with read replicas that can be scaled and one support multi AZ. Which one would you use?
questions.answer: ElastiCache Redis.

questions.question: You need a cache that supports sharding and built with multi-threaded architecture in mind. Which one would you use?
questions.answer: ElastiCache Memcached

questions.question: True or False? ElastiCache doesn't supports IAM authentication
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: What patterns are there for loading data into the cache?
questions.answer:
* Write Through: add or update data in the cache when the data is written to the DB
* Lazy Loading: all the read data is cached
* Session Store: store temporary session data in cache

questions.question: What is AWS Redshift and how is it different than RDS?
questions.answer: cloud data warehouse

questions.question: What do you if you suspect AWS Redshift performs slowly?
questions.answer:
* You can confirm your suspicion by going to AWS Redshift console and see running queries graph. This should tell you if there are any long-running queries.
* If confirmed, you can query for running queries and cancel the irrelevant queries
* Check for connection leaks (query for running connections and include their IP)
* Check for table locks and kill irrelevant locking sessions

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/documentdb)

questions.question: Which (free) tool would you use to get information on cost savings?

questions.question: You would like to have on-perm storage access to AWS storage. What would you use for that?

questions.question: What is Route 53?
questions.answer:
[AWS Route 53](https://aws.amazon.com/route53): "Amazon Route 53 is a highly available and scalable cloud Domain Name System (DNS) web service..."

Some of Route 53 features:
  * Register domains
  * DNS service - domain name translations
  * Health checks - verify your app is available
  * Not a feature but its SLA is 100% availability

questions.question: What it means that "Route 53 is an Authoritative DNS"?
questions.answer: The customer can update DNS records

questions.question: What each Route 53 record contains?
questions.answer:
* Domain/subdomain name (e.g. blipblop.com)
* Value (e.g. 201.7.202.2)
* Record type (e.g. A, AAAA, MX)
* TTL: amount of time the  record is going to be cached
* Routing Policy: how to respond to queries

questions.question: What DNS record types does Route 53 supports?
questions.answer:
* A
* AAAA
* CNAME
* NS
* DS
* CAA
* SOA
* MX
* TXT
* SPF
* SRV
* NAPTR
* PTR

questions.question: What are hosted zones?
questions.answer: A container that includes records for defining how to route traffic from a domain and its subdomains

questions.question: What types of hosted zones are there?
questions.answer:
* Public Hosted Zones - include records to specify how to route traffic on the internet
* Private Hosted Zones - contain records that specify how you traffic within VPC(s)

questions.question: What is the difference between CNAME record and an Alias record?
questions.answer:
CNAME is used for mapping one hostname to any other hostname while Alias is used to map an hostname to an AWS resource.

In addition, Alias work for both root domain (somedomain.com) and non-root domain, while CNAME works only with non-root domain (foo.somedomain.com)

questions.question: True or False? Alias record can be set up for an EC2 DNS name
questions.answer: False

questions.question: True or False? Alias record can be set up for an VPC interface endpoint
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? Alias record is only of type A or AAAA
questions.answer: True

questions.question: What is a routing policy in regards to AWS Route 53?
questions.answer: A routing policy routing defines how Route 53 responds to DNS queries.

questions.question: What Route 53 routing policies are there?
questions.answer:
* Simple 
* Geolocation
* Failover
* Latency based
* Geoproximity
* Multi-Value Answer
* Weighted

questions.question: Suppose you need to route % of your traffic to a certain instance and the rest of the traffic, to another instance. Which routing policy would you choose?
questions.answer: Weighted routing policy.

questions.question: Suppose you need to route traffic to a single source with Route 53, without any other requirements, which routing policy would you choose?
questions.answer: The `simple` routing policy

questions.question: Explain the geolocation routing policy
questions.answer:
* Routing based on user location
* Location can be specified by continent, country or US state
* It's recommended to have a default record in case there is no match on location

questions.question: What are some use cases for using geolocation routing policy?
questions.answer:
* Restrict content distribution
* App localization
* Load balancing

questions.question: Explain the geoproximity routing policy
questions.answer:
* Route based on the geographic location of resources
* Shifting routing is done based on the `bias` value
* Resources can be of AWS and non-AWS type
    * For non-AWS you have to specify latitude and longitude in addition to AWS region as done in AWS-based resources
* To use it, you have to use Route 53 traffic flow

questions.question: What are some use cases for weighted routing policy?
questions.answer:
* Load balancing between regions
* Testing new applications versions

questions.question: True or False? Route 53 simple routing policy supports both single and multiple values
questions.answer:
True.

If multiple values are returned from Route 53 then, the client chooses a single value to use.

questions.question: True or False? In weighted routing DNS records must have the same name but not the same type
questions.answer: False. They must have the same name AND type.

questions.question: You would like to use a routing policy that will take latency into account and will route to the resource with the lowest latency. Which routing policy would you use?
questions.answer: Latency-based routing policy.

questions.question: What happens when you set all records to weight 0 when using Weighted routing policy?
questions.answer: All records are used equally.

questions.question: What Route 53 health checks are used for?
questions.answer:
Automated DNS failover based on monitoring:

  * Another health check
  * endpoint (app, AWS resource, server)
  * CloudWatch alarms

questions.question: You would like to use a routing policy based on the resource location and be able to shift more traffic to some resources. Which one would you use?
questions.answer: Geoproximity routing policy

questions.question: Explain Route 53 Traffic Flow feature
questions.answer:
It's a visual editor for managing complex routing decision trees. It allows you to simplify the process of managing records.

Configuration can be saved (as Traffic Flow Policy) and applied to different domains/hosted zones. In addition, it supports versioning

questions.question: What are calculated health checks?
questions.answer: When you combine the results of multiple health checks into a single health check.

questions.question: What is one possible use case for using calculated health checks?
questions.answer: Performing maintenance for a website without causing all the health checks to fail.

questions.question: You would like to use a routing policy based on the user location. Which one would you use?
questions.answer:
Geolocation routing policy. It's based on user location.

Don't confuse it with latency-based routing policy. While shorter distance may result in lower latency, this is not the requirement in the question.

questions.question: True or False? Route 53 Multi Value is a substitute for those who want cheaper solution than ELB
questions.answer: False. Route 53 Multi Value is not a substitute for ELB. It's focused on client-side load balancing as opposed to ELB.

questions.question: True or False? Domain registrar and DNS service is inherently the same thing
questions.answer: False. DNS service can be Route 53 (where you manage DNS records) while the domain itself can be purchased from other sources that aren't Amazon related (e.g. GoDadday).

questions.question: What is Simple Queue Service (SQS)?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS) is a fully managed message queuing service that enables you to decouple and scale microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications".

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/sqs)

questions.question: Explain "producer" and "consumer" in regards to messaging queue
questions.answer:
Producer is the application or in general, the source that sends messages to the queue.

Consumer is the process or application that pulls the messages from the queue.

questions.question: What "default retention of messages" means?
questions.answer:
It refers to a retention period in which a message has to consumed/processed and deleted from the queue.

As of today, the retention of a message is 4 days by default and the maximum allows is 14 days.

questions.question: What's the limitation on message size in SQS?  * 128KB * 128MB * 256KB * 256MB
questions.answer: 256KB

questions.question: True or False? It's possible to have duplicated messages in the queue
questions.answer: True. It's referred to as "at least once delivery".

questions.question: True or False? "Consumers" can be only EC2 instances
questions.answer: False. They can be Lambda functions and even on-premise instances

questions.question: True or False? Processes/Applications use from the SDK the SendMessage API in order to send messages to the queue
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: What it means "best effort ordering" in regards to SQS?
questions.answer: It means messages in the queue can be out of order.

questions.question: What is "Delay Queue" in regards to SQS?
questions.answer:
It's the time in seconds to delay the delivery of new messages (when they reached the queue already).

The limit as of today is 15 minutes.

questions.question: What is "Visibility Timeout?"
questions.answer:
The time in seconds for a message to not be visible for consumers.

The limit as of today is 12 hours

questions.question: Give an example of architecture or workflow that involves SQS and EC2 & S3
questions.answer:
A website that allows users to upload videos and adds subtitles to them:

1. First the user uploads the video through the web interface which uploads it to an S3 bucket
2. SQS gets notified with a message on the video location
3. EC2 instance (or Lambda function) starts to work on adding the subtitles
4. The video with the subtitles is uploaded to an S3 buckets
5. SQS gets notified of the result and specifically the video location

questions.question: What is Simply Notification Service?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "a highly available, durable, secure, fully managed pub/sub messaging service that enables you to decouple microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications."

Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/sns)

questions.question: Explain the following in regards to SNS:    - Topics   - Subscribers   - Publishers
questions.answer:
* Topics - used for grouping multiple endpoints
  * Subscribers - the endpoints where topics send messages to
  * Publishers - the provider of the message (event, person, ...)

questions.question: How SNS is different from SQS?
questions.answer:
SNS, as opposed to SQS, works in a publisher/subscriber model. Where's SQS works in  Producer/Consumer model.

SQS delivers the message to one consumer where's SNS will send a message to multiple subscribers.

questions.question: What's a Fan-Out pattern?
questions.answer: A messaging pattern where a single message is send to multiple destinations (often simultaneously). So one-to-many broadcast message.

questions.question: What is AWS CloudWatch?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon CloudWatch is a monitoring and observability service..."

More on CloudWatch [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch)

questions.question: What is AWS CloudTrail?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS CloudTrail is a service that enables governance, compliance, operational auditing, and risk auditing of your AWS account."

Read more on CloudTrail [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail)

questions.question: What are Service Control Policies and to what service they belong?
questions.answer:
AWS organizations service and the definition by Amazon: "SCPs offer central control over the maximum available permissions for all accounts in your organization, allowing you to ensure your accounts stay within your organizationŌĆÖs access control guidelines."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scp.html)

questions.question: Explain AWS pricing model
questions.answer:
It mainly works on "pay-as-you-go" meaning you pay only for what are using and when you are using it.
In s3 you pay for 1. How much data you are storing 2. Making requests (PUT, POST, ...)
In EC2 it's based on the purchasing option (on-demand, spot, ...), instance type, AMI type and the region used.

More on AWS pricing model [here](https://aws.amazon.com/pricing)

questions.question: How do you estimate AWS costs?

* TCO calculator

* Cost Explorer
* AWS Budgets
* Cost Allocation Tags

questions.answer:

questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Connect is an easy to use omnichannel cloud contact center that helps companies provide superior customer service at a lower cost."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/connect)

questions.question: What are "APN Consulting Partners"?
questions.answer:

questions.question: Which of the following are AWS accounts types (and are sorted by order)?    - Basic, Developer, Business, Enterprise   - Newbie, Intermediate, Pro, Enterprise   - Developer, Basic, Business, Enterprise   - Beginner, Pro, Intermediate Enterprise
questions.answer: - Basic, Developer, Business, Enterprise

questions.answer: True. You pay differently based on the chosen region.

questions.question: What is "AWS Infrastructure Event Management"?
questions.answer: AWS Definition: "AWS Infrastructure Event Management is a structured program available to Enterprise Support customers (and Business Support customers for an additional fee) that helps you plan for large-scale events such as product or application launches, infrastructure migrations, and marketing events."

Read more on Organizations [here](https://aws.amazon.com/organizations)

questions.question: What's an OU in regards to AWS Organizations?'
questions.answer:
OU (Organizational Units) is a way to group multiple accounts together so you can treat them as a single unit.

By default there is the "Root" OU created in AWS Organizations.

Most of the time OUs are based on functions or common set of controls.

questions.question: What is AWS CodeDeploy?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS CodeDeploy is a fully managed deployment service that automates software deployments to a variety of compute services such as Amazon EC2, AWS Fargate, AWS Lambda, and your on-premises servers."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/codedeploy)

questions.question: Explain what is CloudFormation
questions.answer: AWS definition: "AWS CloudFormation is a service that helps you model and set up your Amazon Web Services resources so that you can spend less time managing those resources and more time focusing on your applications that run in AWS. You create a template that describes all the AWS resources that you want (like Amazon EC2 instances or Amazon RDS DB instances), and CloudFormation takes care of provisioning and configuring those resources for you."

questions.question: What is AWS CDK?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "The AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) is an open-source software development framework to define cloud infrastructure as code and provision it through AWS CloudFormation. CDK gives the flexibility to use popular programming languages like TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java, C# and Go (in Developer Preview) to define your infrastructure, and AWS CDK provides a set of libraries for AWS services that abstract away the need to write raw CloudFormation templates.

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cdk)

questions.question: What is AWS Lightsail?
questions.answer: AWS definition: "Lightsail is an easy-to-use cloud platform that offers you everything needed to build an application or website, plus a cost-effective, monthly plan."

questions.question: What is AWS Rekognition?

AWS definition: "Amazon Rekognition makes it easy to add image and video analysis to your applications using proven, highly scalable, deep learning technology that requires no machine learning expertise to use."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/rekognition)

questions.question: What AWS Resource Groups used for?

Amazon definition: "You can use resource groups to organize your AWS resources. Resource groups make it easier to manage and automate tasks on large numbers of resources at one time. "

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ARG/latest/userguide/welcome.html)

questions.question: What is AWS Global Accelerator?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Global Accelerator is a service that improves the availability and performance of your applications with local or global users..."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/global-accelerator)

questions.question: What is AWS Config?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Config is a service that enables you to assess, audit, and evaluate the configurations of your AWS resources."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/config)

questions.question: What is AWS X-Ray?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS X-Ray helps developers analyze and debug production, distributed applications, such as those built using a microservices architecture."
Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/xray)

questions.question: What is AWS OpsWorks?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS OpsWorks is a configuration management service that provides managed instances of Chef and Puppet."

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/opsworks)

questions.question: What is AWS Snowmobile?
questions.answer:
"AWS Snowmobile is an Exabyte-scale data transfer service used to move extremely large amounts of data to AWS."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/snowmobile)

questions.question: What is AWS Athena?
questions.answer:
"Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that makes it easy to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL."

Learn more about AWS Athena [here](https://aws.amazon.com/athena)

questions.question: What is Amazon Cloud Directory?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Cloud Directory is a highly available multi-tenant directory-based store in AWS. These directories scale automatically to hundreds of millions of objects as needed for applications."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/clouddirectory/latest/developerguide/what_is_cloud_directory.html)

questions.question: What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Elastic Beanstalk is an easy-to-use service for deploying and scaling web applications and services...You can simply upload your code and Elastic Beanstalk automatically handles the deployment"

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk)

questions.question: What is AWS SWF?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon SWF helps developers build, run, and scale background jobs that have parallel or sequential steps. You can think of Amazon SWF as a fully-managed state tracker and task coordinator in the Cloud."

Learn more on Amazon Simple Workflow Service [here](https://aws.amazon.com/swf)

questions.question: What is AWS EMR?

AWS definition: "big data platform for processing vast amounts of data using open source tools such as Apache Spark, Apache Hive, Apache HBase, Apache Flink, Apache Hudi, and Presto."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/emr)

questions.question: What is AWS Quick Starts?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "Quick Starts are built by AWS solutions architects and partners to help you deploy popular technologies on AWS, based on AWS best practices for security and high availability."

Read more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/quickstart)

questions.question: What is the Trusted Advisor?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Trusted Advisor provides recommendations that help you follow AWS best practices. Trusted Advisor evaluates your account by using checks. These checks identify ways to optimize your AWS infrastructure, improve security and performance, reduce costs, and monitor service quotas."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/technology/trusted-advisor/)

questions.question: What is AWS Service Catalog?
