Amazon definition: "AWS Service Catalog allows organizations to create and manage catalogs of IT services that are approved for use on AWS."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/servicecatalog)

questions.question: What is AWS CAF?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Professional Services created the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) to help organizations design and travel an accelerated path to successful cloud adoption. "

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/professional-services/CAF)

questions.question: What is AWS Cloud9?
questions.answer: AWS: "AWS Cloud9 is a cloud-based integrated development environment (IDE) that lets you write, run, and debug your code with just a browser"

questions.question: What is AWS CloudShell?
questions.answer: AWS: "AWS CloudShell is a browser-based shell that makes it easy to securely manage, explore, and interact with your AWS resources."

questions.question: What is AWS Application Discovery Service?
questions.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Application Discovery Service helps enterprise customers plan migration projects by gathering information about their on-premises data centers."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/application-discovery)

questions.question: What is the AWS well-architected framework and what pillars it's based on?
questions.answer:
AWS definition: "The Well-Architected Framework has been developed to help cloud architects build secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure for their applications. Based on five pillars ŌĆö operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, and cost optimization"

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/architecture/well-architected)

questions.question: What AWS services are serverless (or have the option to be serverless)?
questions.answer:
AWS Lambda
AWS Athena

questions.question: What high availability means from AWS perspective?
questions.answer:
* Application/Service is running in at least 2 availability zones
* Application/Service should survive (= operate as usual) a data center disaster

questions.question: Describe in high-level how to upgrade a system on AWS with (near) zero downtime
questions.answer:
One way is through launching a new instance. In more detail:

1. Launch a new instance
2. Install all the updates and applications
3. Test the instance
4. If all tests passed successfully, you can start using the new instance and perform the switch with the old one, in one of various ways:
  1. Go to route53 and update the record with the IP of the new instance
  2. If you are using an Elastic IP then move it to the new instance
  ...

questions.question: You try to use an detached EBS volume from us-east-1b in us-east-1a, but it fails. What might be the reason?
questions.answer: EBS volumes are locked to a specific availability zone. To use them in another availability zone, you need to take a snapshot and restore it in the destination availability zone.

questions.question: When you launch EC2 instances, it takes them time to boot due to commands you run with user data. How to improve instances boot time?
questions.answer: Consider creating customized AMI with the commands from user data already executed there. This will allow you launch instance instantly.

questions.question: You try to mount EFS on your EC2 instance and it doesn't work (hangs...) What might be a possible reason?
questions.answer: Security group isn't attached to your EFS or it lacks a rule to allow NFS traffic.

questions.question: How to migrate an EBS volume across availability zones?
questions.answer:
1. Pause the application
2. Take a snapshot of the EBS volume
3. Restore the snapshot in another availability zone

questions.question: How to encrypt an unencrypted EBS volume attached to an EC2 instance?
questions.answer:
1. Create EBS snapshot of the volume
2. Copy the snapshot and mark the "Encrypt" option
3. Create a new EBS volume out of the encrypted snapshot

questions.question: You've created a network load balancer but it doesn't work (you can't reach your app on your EC2 instance). What might be a possible reason?
questions.answer:
Missing security group or misconfigured one.
For example, if you go to your instances in the AWS console you might see that the instances under your NLB are in "unhealthy status" and if you didn't create a dedicated security group for your NLB, that means that the security group used is the one attached to the EC2 instances.

Go to the security group of your instance(s) and enable the traffic that NLB should forward (e.g. TCP on port 80).

questions.question: You have a load balancer running and behind it 5 web servers. Users complain that every time they move to a new page, they have to authenticate, instead of doing it once. How can you solve it?
questions.answer: Enable sticky sessions. This way, the user keep working against the same instance, instead of being redirected to a different instance every request.

questions.question: You have a load balancer running and behind it 5 web servers. Users complain that some times when they try to use the application it doesn't works. You've found out that sometimes some of the instances crash. How would you deal with it?
questions.answer: One possible way is to use health checks with the load balancer to ensure the instances are ready to be used before forwarding traffic to them.

questions.question: You run your application on 5 EC2 instances on one AZ and on 10 EC2 instances in another AZ. You distribute traffic between all of them using a network load balancer, but it seems that instances in one AZ have higher CPU rates than the instances in the other AZ. What might be the issue and how to solve it?
questions.answer:
It's possible that traffic is distributed evenly between the AZs but that doesn't mean it's distributed equally across all instances evenly.

To distribute it evenly between all the instances, you have to enable cross-zone load balancing.

questions.question: You are running an ALB that routes traffic using two hostnames: a.b.com and d.e.com. Is it possible to configure HTTPS for both of the hostnames?
questions.answer: Yes, using SNI (Server Name Indication) each application can has its own SSL certificate (This is supported from 2017).

questions.question: You have set up read replicas to scale reads but users complain that when they update posts in forums, the posts are not being updated. What may cause this issue?
questions.answer: Read Replicas use asynchronous replication so it's possible users access a read replica instance that wasn't synced yet.

questions.question: You need a persistent shared storage between your containers that some are running in Fargate and some in ECS. What would you use?
questions.answer: EFS. It allows us to have persistent multi-AZ shared storage for containers.

questions.question: You would like to run an AWS Fargate task every time a file is uploaded to a certain S3 bucket. How would you achieve that?
questions.answer:
Use Amazon EventBridge so every time a file is uploaded to an S3 bucket (event) it will run an ECS task.

Such task should have an ECS Task Role so it can get the object from the S3 bucket (and possibly other permissions if it needs to update the DB for example).

questions.question: Your hosts scale down and then back up quite often. What's your take on that?
questions.answer: Often circular scaling (scale down, up and vice versa) is not a sign that the threshold set for scaling down and up are met quite often. In most cases that's a sign for you to adjust the threshold so scaling down doesn't happen as often.

questions.question: You've been asked to design an architecture for high performance and low-latency application (millions of requests per second). Which load balancer would you use?
questions.answer: Network Load Balancer

questions.question: What should you use for scaling reads?
questions.answer: You can use an ElastiCache cluster or RDS Read Replicas.

questions.question: You have two applications who communicate synchronously. It worked fine until there suddenly a spike of traffic. What change you might apply in this case?
questions.answer:
More details are missing to determine for sure but it might be better to decouple the applications by introducing one of the following:

* Queue model with SQS
* Publisher/Subscriber model with SNS

questions.question: What's an ARN?
questions.answer:
ARN (Amazon Resources Names) are used for uniquely identifying different AWS resources.
It is used when you would like to identify resource uniqely across all AWS infrastructures.

questions.question: What is Kubernetes? Why organizations are using it?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes is an open-source system that provides users with the ability to manage, scale and deploy containerized applications.

To understand what Kubernetes is good for, let's look at some examples:

* You would like to run a certain application in a container on multiple different locations and sync changes across all of them, no matter where they run
* Performing updates and changes across hundreds of containers
* Handle cases where the current load requires to scale up (or down)

questions.question: When or why NOT to use Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
- If you manage low level infrastructure or baremetals, Kubernetes is probably not what you need or want
  - If you are a small team (like less than 20 engineers) running less than a dozen of containers, Kubernetes might be an overkill (even if you need scale, rolling out updates, etc.). You might still enjoy the benefits of using managed Kubernetes, but you definitely want to think about it carefully before making a decision on whether to adopt it.

questions.question: What are some of Kubernetes features?
questions.answer:
- Self-Healing: Kubernetes uses health checks to monitor containers and run certain actions upon failure or other type of events, like restarting the container
  - Load Balancing: Kubernetes can split and/or balance requests to applications running in the cluster, based on the state of the Pods running the application
  - Operators: Kubernetes packaged applications that can use the API of the cluster to update its state and trigger actions based on events and application state changes
  - Automated Rollout: Gradual updates roll out to applications and support in roll back in case anything goes wrong
  - Scaling: Scaling horizontally (down and up) based on different state parameters and custom defined criteria
  - Secrets: you have a mechanism for storing user names, passwords and service endpoints in a private way, where not everyone using the cluster are able to view it

questions.question: What Kubernetes objects are there?
questions.answer:
* Pod
  * Service
  * ReplicationController
  * ReplicaSet
  * DaemonSet
  * Namespace
  * ConfigMap
  ...

questions.question: What fields are mandatory with any Kubernetes object?
questions.answer: metadata, kind and apiVersion

questions.question: What is kubectl?
questions.answer: Kubectl is the Kubernetes command line tool that allows you to run commands against Kubernetes clusters. For example, you can use kubectl to deploy applications, inspect and manage cluster resources, and view logs.

questions.question: What Kubernetes objects do you usually use when deploying applications in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
* Deployment - creates the Pods () and watches them
* Service: route traffic to Pods internally
* Ingress: route traffic from outside the cluster

questions.question: Why there is no such command in Kubernetes? kubectl get containers
questions.answer: Becaused container is not a Kubernetes object. The smallest object unit in Kubernetes is a Pod. In a single Pod you can find one or more containers.

questions.question: What actions or operations you consider as best practices when it comes to Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
- Always make sure Kubernetes YAML files are valid. Applying automated checks and pipelines is recommended.
  - Always specify requests and limits to prevent situation where containers are using the entire cluster memory which may lead to OOM issue
  - Specify labels to logically group Pods, Deployments, etc. Use labels to identify the type of the application for example, among other things

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes Cluster?
questions.answer:
Red Hat Definition: "A Kubernetes cluster is a set of node machines for running containerized applications. If youŌĆÖre running Kubernetes, youŌĆÖre running a cluster.
At a minimum, a cluster contains a worker node and a master node."

Read more [here](https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/containers/what-is-a-kubernetes-cluster)

questions.question: What is a Node?
questions.answer:
A node is a virtual or a physical machine that serves as a worker for running the applications.
It's recommended to have at least 3 nodes in a production environment.

questions.question: What the master node is responsible for?
questions.answer:
The master coordinates all the workflows in the cluster:

* Scheduling applications
* Managing desired state
* Rolling out new updates

questions.question: Describe shortly and in high-level, what happens when you run kubectl get nodes
questions.answer:
1. Your user is getting authenticated
2. Request is validated by the kube-apiserver
3. Data is retrieved from etcd

questions.question: True or False? Every cluster must have 0 or more master nodes and at least 1 worker
questions.answer: False. A Kubernetes cluster consists of at least 1 master and can have 0 workers (although that wouldn't be very useful...)

questions.question: What are the components of the master node (aka control plane)?
questions.answer:
* API Server - the Kubernetes API. All cluster components communicate through it
  * Scheduler - assigns an application with a worker node it can run on
  * Controller Manager - cluster maintenance (replications, node failures, etc.)
  * etcd - stores cluster configuration

questions.question: What are the components of a worker node (aka data plane)?
questions.answer:
* Kubelet - an agent responsible for node communication with the master.
  * Kube-proxy - load balancing traffic between app components
  * Container runtime - the engine runs the containers (Podman, Docker, ...)

questions.question: You are managing multiple Kubernetes clusters. How do you quickly change between the clusters using kubectl?
questions.answer: `kubectl config use-context`

questions.question: How do you prevent high memory usage in your Kubernetes cluster and possibly issues like memory leak and OOM?
questions.answer: Apply requests and limits, especially on third party applications (where the uncertainty is even bigger)

questions.question: Do you have experience with deploying a Kubernetes cluster? If so, can you describe the process in high-level?
questions.answer:
1. Create multiple instances you will use as Kubernetes nodes/workers. Create also an instance to act as the Master. The instances can be provisioned in a cloud or they can be virtual machines on bare metal hosts.
2. Provision a certificate authority that will be used to generate TLS certificates for the different components of a Kubernetes cluster (kubelet, etcd, ...)
  1. Generate a certificate and private key for the different components
3. Generate kubeconfigs so the different clients of Kubernetes can locate the API servers and authenticate.
4. Generate encryption key that will be used for encrypting the cluster data
5. Create an etcd cluster

questions.question: Which command will list all the object types in a cluster?
questions.answer: `kubectl api-resources`

questions.question: What kubectl get componentstatus does?
questions.answer: Outputs the status of each of the control plane components.

questions.question: What happens to running pods if if you stop Kubelet on the worker nodes?
questions.answer: When you stop the kubelet service on a worker node, it will no longer be able to communicate with the Kubernetes API server. As a result, the node will be marked as NotReady and the pods running on that node will be marked as Unknown. The Kubernetes control plane will then attempt to reschedule the pods to other available nodes in the cluster.

questions.question: Run a command to view all nodes of the cluster
questions.answer:
`kubectl get nodes`

Note: You might want to create an alias (`alias k=kubectl`) and get used to `k get no`

questions.question: Create a list of all nodes in JSON format and store it in a file called "some_nodes.json"
questions.answer: `k get nodes -o json > some_nodes.json`

questions.question: Check what labels one of your nodes in the cluster has
questions.answer: `k get no minikube --show-labels`

questions.question: Explain what is a Pod
questions.answer:
A Pod is a group of one or more containers, with shared storage and network resources, and a specification for how to run the containers.

Pods are the smallest deployable units of computing that you can create and manage in Kubernetes.

questions.question: Deploy a pod called "my-pod" using the nginx:alpine image
questions.answer:
`kubectl run my-pod --image=nginx:alpine`

If you are a Kubernetes beginner you should know that this is not a common way to run Pods. The common way is to run a Deployment which in turn runs Pod(s).

In addition, Pods and/or Deployments are usually defined in files rather than executed directly using only the CLI arguments.

questions.question: What are your thoughts on "Pods are not meant to be created directly"?
questions.answer:
Pods are usually indeed not created directly. You'll notice that Pods are usually created as part of another entities such as Deployments or ReplicaSets.

If a Pod dies, Kubernetes will not bring it back. This is why it's more useful for example to define ReplicaSets that will make sure that a given number of Pods will always run, even after a certain Pod dies.

questions.question: How many containers can a pod contain?
questions.answer:
A pod can include multiple containers but in most cases it would probably be one container per pod.

There are some patterns where it makes to run more than one container like the "side-car" pattern where you might want to perform logging or some other operation that is executed by another container running with your app container in the same Pod.

questions.question: What use cases exist for running multiple containers in a single pod?
questions.answer:
A web application with separate (= in their own containers) logging and monitoring components/adapters is one examples.
A CI/CD pipeline (using Tekton for example) can run multiple containers in one Pod if a Task contains multiple commands.

questions.question: What are the possible Pod phases?
questions.answer:
* Running - The Pod bound to a node and at least one container is running
  * Failed/Error - At least one container in the Pod terminated with a failure
  * Succeeded - Every container in the Pod terminated with success
  * Unknown - Pod's state could not be obtained
  * Pending - Containers are not yet running (Perhaps images are still being downloaded or the pod wasn't scheduled yet)

questions.question: True or False? By default, pods are isolated. This means they are unable to receive traffic from any source
questions.answer: False. By default, pods are non-isolated = pods accept traffic from any source.

questions.question: True or False? The "Pending" phase means the Pod was not yet accepted by the Kubernetes cluster so the scheduler can't run it unless it's accepted
questions.answer: False. "Pending" is after the Pod was accepted by the cluster, but the container can't run for different reasons like images not yet downloaded.

questions.question: True or False? A single Pod can be split across multiple nodes
questions.answer: False. A single Pod can run on a single node.

questions.question: You run a pod and you see the status ContainerCreating
questions.answer:
The `ContainerCreating` status means the Pod has been scheduled to a node, but the container runtime hasn't started all containers yet. Common causes:

1. **Image pull** ŌĆö The container image is being pulled (large image or slow registry)
2. **Volume mounting** ŌĆö A PersistentVolume is being attached/provisioned
3. **Init containers** ŌĆö Init containers are still running
4. **Container runtime delay** ŌĆö CRI (containerd/CRI-O) is starting the container

**Debugging:**
```bash
kubectl describe pod    # Check Events section for pull/volume errors
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=
```

**Common issues:**
- ImagePullBackOff: wrong image name, registry auth, network
- PVC Pending: no matching PV, storage class issues
- CrashLoopBackOff after ContainerCreating: container starts but immediately exits

questions.question: True or False? A volume defined in Pod can be accessed by all the containers of that Pod
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: What happens when you run a Pod with kubectl?
questions.answer:
1. Kubectl sends a request to the API server (kube-apiserver) to create the Pod
   1. In the the process the user gets authenticated and the request is being validated.
   2. etcd is being updated with the data
2. The Scheduler detects that there is an unassigned Pod by monitoring the API server (kube-apiserver)
3. The Scheduler chooses a node to assign the Pod to
   1. etcd is being updated with the information
4. The Scheduler updates the API server about which node it chose
5. Kubelet (which also monitors the API server) notices there is a Pod assigned to the same node on which it runs and that Pod isn't running
6. Kubelet sends request to the container engine (e.g. Docker) to create and run the containers
7. An update is sent by Kubelet to the API server (notifying it that the Pod is running)
   1. etcd is being updated by the API server again

questions.question: How to confirm a container is running after running the command kubectl run web --image nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged
questions.answer:
* When you run `kubectl describe pods ` it will tell whether the container is running:
`Status:       Running`
* Run a command inside the container: `kubectl exec web -- ls`

questions.question: After running kubectl run database --image mongo you see the status is "CrashLoopBackOff". What could possibly went wrong and what do you do to confirm?
questions.answer:
"CrashLoopBackOff" means the Pod is starting, crashing, starting...and so it repeats itself.
There are many different reasons to get this error - lack of permissions, init-container misconfiguration, persistent volume connection issue, etc.

One of the ways to check why it happened it to run `kubectl describe po ` and having a look at the exit code

```
 Last State:     Terminated
   Reason:       Error
   Exit Code:    100
```

Another way to check what's going on, is to run `kubectl logs `. This will provide us with the logs from the containers running in that Pod.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the following lines  ``` livenessProbe:   exec:     command:     - cat     - /appStatus   initialDelaySeconds: 10   periodSeconds: 5 ```
questions.answer:
These lines make use of `liveness probe`. It's used to restart a container when it reaches a non-desired state.
In this case, if the command `cat /appStatus` fails, Kubernetes will kill the container and will apply the restart policy. The `initialDelaySeconds: 10` means that Kubelet will wait 10 seconds before running the command/probe for the first time. From that point on, it will run it every 5 seconds, as defined with `periodSeconds`

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the following lines  ``` readinessProbe:       tcpSocket:         port: 2017       initialDelaySeconds: 15       periodSeconds: 20 ```
questions.answer: They define a readiness probe where the Pod will not be marked as "Ready" before it will be possible to connect to port 2017 of the container. The first check/probe will start after 15 seconds from the moment the container started to run and will continue to run the check/probe every 20 seconds until it will manage to connect to the defined port.

questions.question: What does the "ErrImagePull" status of a Pod means?
questions.answer:
It wasn't able to pull the image specified for running the container(s). This can happen if the client didn't authenticated for example.
More details can be obtained with `kubectl describe po `.

questions.question: What happens when you delete a Pod?
questions.answer:
1. The `TERM` signal is sent to kill the main processes inside the containers of the given Pod
2. Each container is given a period of 30 seconds to shut down the processes gracefully
3. If the grace period expires, the `KILL` signal is used to kill the processes forcefully and the containers as well

questions.question: Explain liveness probes
questions.answer:
Liveness probes is a useful mechanism used for restarting the container when a certain check/probe, the user has defined, fails.
For example, the user can define that the command `cat /app/status` will run every X seconds and the moment this command fails, the container will be restarted.

You can read more about it in [kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes)

questions.question: Explain readiness probes
questions.answer:
readiness probes used by Kubelet to know when a container is ready to start running, accepting traffic.
For example, a readiness probe can be to connect port 8080 on a container. Once Kubelet manages to connect it, the Pod is marked as ready

You can read more about it in [kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes)

questions.question: How readiness probe status affect Services when they are combined?
questions.answer: Only containers whose state set to Success will be able to receive requests sent to the Service.

questions.question: Why it's common to have only one container per Pod in most cases?
questions.answer: One reason is that it makes it harder to scale when you need to scale only one of the containers in a given Pod.

questions.question: True or False? Once a Pod is assisgned to a worker node, it will only run on that node, even if it fails at some point and spins up a new Pod
questions.answer: True.

questions.question: True or False? Each Pod, when created, gets its own public IP address
questions.answer:
False. Each Pod gets an IP address but an internal one and not publicly accessible.

To make a Pod externally accessible, we need to use an object called Service in Kubernetes.

questions.question: What are Static Pods?
questions.answer: [Kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/static-pod/): "Static Pods are managed directly by the kubelet daemon on a specific node, without the API server observing them. Unlike Pods that are managed by the control plane (for example, a Deployment); instead, the kubelet watches each static Pod (and restarts it if it fails)."

questions.question: True or False? The same as there are "Static Pods" there are other static resources like "deployments" and "replicasets"
questions.answer: False.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using Static Pods?
questions.answer: One clear use case is running Control Plane Pods -  running Pods such as kube-apiserver, scheduler, etc. These should run and operate regardless of whether some components of the cluster work or not and they should run on specific nodes of the cluster.

questions.question: How to identify which Pods are Static Pods?
questions.answer:
The suffix of the Pods is the same as the name of the nodes on which they are running
TODO: check if it's always the case.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a static pod?:  * kube-scheduler * kube-proxy * kube-apiserver
questions.answer: kube-proxy - it's a DaemonSet (since it has to be presented on every node in the cluster). There is no one specific node on which it has to run.

questions.question: Where static Pods manifests are located?
questions.answer:
Most of the time it's in /etc/kubernetes/manifests but you can verify with `grep -i static /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` to locate the value of `statisPodsPath`.

It might be that your config is in different path. To verify run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` and see what is the value of --config argument of the process `/usr/bin/kubelet`

The key itself for defining the path of static Pods is `staticPodPath`. So if your config is in `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` you can run `grep staticPodPath /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`.

questions.question: Describe how would you delete a static Pod
questions.answer:
Locate the static Pods directory (look at `staticPodPath` in kubelet configuration file).

Go to that directory and remove the manifest/definition of the staic Pod (`rm /`)

questions.question: How to check to which worker node the pods were scheduled to? In other words, how to check on which node a certain Pod is running?
questions.answer: `kubectl get pods -o wide`

questions.question: How to delete a pod?
questions.answer: `kubectl delete pod pod_name`

questions.question: List all the pods with the label "env=prod"
questions.answer:
`k get po -l env=prod`

To count them: `k get po -l env=prod --no-headers | wc -l`

questions.question: How to list the pods in the current namespace?
questions.answer: `kubectl get po`

questions.question: How view all the pods running in all the namespaces?
questions.answer: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces`

questions.question: You try to run a Pod but it's in "Pending" state. What might be the reason?
questions.answer: One possible reason is that the scheduler which supposed to schedule Pods on nodes, is not running. To verify it, you can run `kubectl get po -A | grep scheduler` or check directly in `kube-system` namespace.

questions.question: What kubectl logs [pod-name] command does?
questions.answer: Prints the logs for a container in a pod.

questions.question: What kubectl describe pod [pod name] does? command does?
questions.answer: Show details of a specific resource or group of resources.

questions.question: Create a static pod with the image python that runs the command sleep 2017
questions.answer:
First change to the directory tracked by kubelet for creating static pod: `cd /etc/kubernetes/manifests` (you can verify path by reading kubelet conf file)

Now create the definition/manifest in that directory
`k run some-pod --image=python --command sleep 2017 --restart=Never --dry-run=client -o yaml > statuc-pod.yaml`

questions.question: Explain Labels
questions.answer: [Kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/): "Labels are key/value pairs that are attached to objects, such as pods. Labels are intended to be used to specify identifying attributes of objects that are meaningful and relevant to users, but do not directly imply semantics to the core system. Labels can be used to organize and to select subsets of objects. Labels can be attached to objects at creation time and subsequently added and modified at any time. Each object can have a set of key/value labels defined. Each Key must be unique for a given object."

questions.question: Explain selectors
questions.answer:
[Kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/#label-selectors): "Unlike names and UIDs, labels do not provide uniqueness. In general, we expect many objects to carry the same label(s).

Via a label selector, the client/user can identify a set of objects. The label selector is the core grouping primitive in Kubernetes.

The API currently supports two types of selectors: equality-based and set-based. A label selector can be made of multiple requirements which are comma-separated. In the case of multiple requirements, all must be satisfied so the comma separator acts as a logical AND (&&) operator."

questions.question: Provide some actual examples of how labels are used
questions.answer:
* Can be used by the scheduler to place certain Pods (with certain labels) on specific nodes
* Used by replicasets to track pods which have to be scaled

questions.question: What are Annotations?
questions.answer: [Kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations/): "You can use Kubernetes annotations to attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata to objects. Clients such as tools and libraries can retrieve this metadata."

questions.question: How annotations different from labels?
questions.answer: [Kuberenets.io](Labels can be used to select objects and to find collections of objects that satisfy certain conditions. In contrast, annotations are not used to identify and select objects. The metadata in an annotation can be small or large, structured or unstructured, and can include characters not permitted by labels.): "Labels can be used to select objects and to find collections of objects that satisfy certain conditions. In contrast, annotations are not used to identify and select objects. The metadata in an annotation can be small or large, structured or unstructured, and can include characters not permitted by labels."

questions.question: How to view the logs of a container running in a Pod?
questions.answer: `k logs POD_NAME`

questions.question: There are two containers inside a Pod called "some-pod". What will happen if you run kubectl logs some-pod
questions.answer: It won't work because there are two containers inside the Pod and you need to specify one of them with `kubectl logs POD_NAME -c CONTAINER_NAME`

questions.question: What is a "Deployment" in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
A┬ĀKubernetes Deployment┬Āis used to tell┬ĀKubernetes┬Āhow to create or modify instances of the pods that hold a containerized application.
Deployments┬Ācan scale the number of replica pods, enable rollout of updated code in a controlled manner, or roll back to an earlier┬Ādeployment┬Āversion if necessary. 

A Deployment is a declarative statement for the desired state for Pods and Replica Sets.

questions.question: How to create a deployment with the image "nginx:alpine"?
questions.answer:
`kubectl create deployment my-first-deployment --image=nginx:alpine`

OR

```
cat << EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:alpine
```

questions.question: How to verify a deployment was created?
questions.answer:
`kubectl get deployments` or `kubectl get deploy`

This command lists all the Deployment objects created and exist in the cluster. It doesn't mean the deployments are readt and running. This can be checked with the "READY" and "AVAILABLE" columns.

questions.question: How to edit a deployment?
questions.answer: `kubectl edit deployment `

questions.question: What happens after you edit a deployment and change the image?
questions.answer:
The pod will terminate and another, new pod, will be created.

Also, when looking at the replicaset, you'll see the old replica doesn't have any pods and a new replicaset is created.

questions.question: How to delete a deployment?
questions.answer:
One way is by specifying the deployment name: `kubectl delete deployment [deployment_name]`

Another way is using the deployment configuration file: `kubectl delete -f deployment.yaml`

questions.question: What happens when you delete a deployment?
questions.answer: The pod related to the deployment will terminate and the replicaset will be removed.

questions.question: What happens behind the scenes when you create a Deployment object?
questions.answer:
The following occurs when you run `kubectl create deployment some_deployment --image=nginx`

1. HTTP request sent to kubernetes API server on the cluster to create a new deployment
2. A new Pod object is created and scheduled to one of the workers nodes
3. Kublet on the worker node notices the new Pod and instructs the Container runtime engine to pull the image from the registry
4. A new container is created using the image that was just pulled

questions.question: How make an app accessible on private or external network?
questions.answer: Using a Service.

questions.question: Can you use a Deployment for stateful applications?
questions.answer:
Technically yes, but it's **not recommended** for most stateful workloads. Deployments assume all Pods are identical and interchangeable ŌĆö which often doesn't hold for stateful apps.

**Why Deployments are problematic for stateful apps:**
- All Pods share the same PVC template ŌĆö each Pod gets a separate volume, but they're indistinguishably named (random suffix)
- No stable network identity ŌĆö Pod names are `-`, making peer discovery difficult for clustered databases
- No ordered scaling/updates ŌĆö Deployment scales up/down all at once, which can corrupt clustered databases (e.g., Cassandra, Kafka)
- Rolling updates may break quorum in clustered stateful apps

**Use StatefulSet instead:**
| Feature | Deployment | StatefulSet |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Pod identity | Random hash | Predictable ordinal (`pod-0`, `pod-1`, ...) |
| PVC management | One PVC template for all Pods | Each Pod gets its own PVC (volumeClaimTemplate) |
| Scaling | All pods created/destroyed simultaneously | Sequential (0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ2, or 2ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ0) |
| Network identity | Random | Stable (`pod-0.service.namespace.svc.cluster.local`) |

**When Deployment IS acceptable:** Single-instance stateful apps (one replica with a PV) where you don't need clustering. Example: a single Postgres instance for a dev environment.

questions.question: Fix the following deployment manifest  ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deploy metadata:   creationTimestamp: null   labels:     app: dep   name: dep spec:   replicas: 3   selector:     matchLabels:       app: dep   strategy: {}   template:     metadata:       creationTimestamp: null       labels:         app: dep     spec:       containers:       - image: redis         name: redis         resources: {} status: {} ```
questions.answer: Change `kind: Deploy` to `kind: Deployment`

questions.question: Fix the following deployment manifest  ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata:   creationTimestamp: null   labels:     app: dep   name: dep spec:   replicas: 3   selector:     matchLabels:       app: depdep   strategy: {}   template:     metadata:       creationTimestamp: null       labels:         app: dep     spec:       containers:       - image: redis         name: redis         resources: {} status: {} ```
questions.answer: The selector doesn't match the label (dep vs depdep). To solve it, fix depdep so it's dep instead.

questions.question: Create a file definition/manifest of a deployment called "dep", with 3 replicas that uses the image 'redis'
questions.answer: `k create deploy dep -o yaml --image=redis --dry-run=client --replicas 3 > deployment.yaml `

questions.question: Delete the deployment `depdep`
questions.answer: `k delete deploy depdep`

questions.question: Create a deployment called "pluck" using the image "redis" and make sure it runs 5 replicas
questions.answer:
`kubectl create deployment pluck --image=redis`

`kubectl scale deployment pluck --replicas=5`

questions.question: Create a deployment with the following properties:  * called "blufer" * using the image "python" * runs 3 replicas * all pods will be placed on a node that has the label "blufer"
questions.answer:
`kubectl create deployment blufer --image=python --replicas=3 -o yaml --dry-run=client > deployment.yaml`

Add the following section (`vi deployment.yaml`):

```
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedlingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: blufer
            operator: Exists
```

`kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml`

questions.question: What is a Service in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
"An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of Pods as a network service." - read more [here](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service)

In simpler words, it allows you to add an internal or external connectivity to a certain application running in a container.

questions.question: How to create a service for an existing deployment called "alle" on port 8080 so the Pod(s) accessible via a Load Balancer?
questions.answer:
The imperative way:

`kubectl expose deployment alle --type=LoadBalancer --port 8080`

questions.question: True or False? The lifecycle of Pods and Services isn't connected so when a Pod dies, the Service still stays
questions.answer: True

questions.question: After creating a service, how to check it was created?
questions.answer: `kubectl get svc`

questions.question: What's the default Service type?
questions.answer: ClusterIP - used for internal communication.

questions.question: What Service types are there?
questions.answer:
* ClusterIP
* NodePort
* LoadBalancer
* ExternalName

More on this topic [here](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/#publishing-services-service-types)

questions.question: How Service and Deployment are connected?
questions.answer: The truth is they aren't connected. Service points to Pod(s) directly, without connecting to the Deployment in any way.

questions.question: What are important steps in defining/adding a Service?
questions.answer:
1. Making sure that targetPort of the Service is matching the containerPort of the Pod
2. Making sure that selector matches at least one of the Pod's labels

questions.question: What is the default service type in Kubernetes and what is it used for?
questions.answer: The default is ClusterIP and it's used for exposing a port internally. It's useful when you want to enable internal communication between Pods and prevent any external access.

questions.question: How to get information on a certain service?
questions.answer:
`kubctl describe service `

It's more common to use `kubectl describe svc ...`

questions.question: What the following command does?  ``` kubectl expose rs some-replicaset --name=replicaset-svc --target-port=2017 --type=NodePort ```
questions.answer: It exposes a ReplicaSet by creating a service called 'replicaset-svc'. The exposed port is 2017 (this is the port used by the application) and the service type is NodePort which means it will be reachable externally.

questions.question: True or False? the target port, in the case of running the following command, will be exposed only on one of the Kubernetes cluster nodes but it will routed to all the pods  ``` kubectl expose rs some-replicaset --name=replicaset-svc --target-port=2017 --type=NodePort ```
questions.answer: False. It will be exposed on every node of the cluster and will be routed to one of the Pods (which belong to the ReplicaSet)

questions.question: How to verify that a certain service configured to forward the requests to a given pod
questions.answer: Run `kubectl describe service` and see if the IPs from "Endpoints" match any IPs from the output of `kubectl get pod -o wide`

questions.question: Explain what will happen when running apply on the following block  ``` apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata:   name: some-app spec:   type: NodePort   ports:   - port: 8080     nodePort: 2017     protocol: TCP   selector:     type: backend     service: some-app ```
questions.answer:
It creates a new Service of the type "NodePort" which means it can be used for internal and external communication with the app.
The port of the application is 8080 and the requests will forwarded to this port. The exposed port is 2017. As a note, this is not a common practice, to specify the nodePort.
The port used TCP (instead of UDP) and this is also the default so you don't have to specify it.
The selector used by the Service to know to which Pods to forward the requests. In this case, Pods with the label "type: backend" and "service: some-app".

questions.question: How to turn the following service into an external one?  ``` spec:   selector:     app: some-app   ports:     - protocol: TCP       port: 8081       targetPort: 8081 ```
questions.answer:
Adding `type: LoadBalancer` and `nodePort`

```
spec:
  selector:
    app: some-app
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8081
      targetPort: 8081
      nodePort: 32412
```

questions.question: What would you use to route traffic from outside the Kubernetes cluster to services within a cluster?
questions.answer: Ingress

questions.question: True or False? When "NodePort" is used, "ClusterIP" will be created automatically?
questions.answer: True

questions.question: When would you use the "LoadBalancer" type
questions.answer: Mostly when you would like to combine it with cloud provider's load balancer

questions.question: How would you map a service to an external address?
questions.answer: Using the 'ExternalName' directive.

questions.question: Describe in detail what happens when you create a service
questions.answer:
1. Kubectl sends a request to the API server to create a Service
2. The controller detects there is a new Service
3. Endpoint objects created with the same name as the service, by the controller
4. The controller is using the Service selector to identify the endpoints
5. kube-proxy detects there is a new endpoint object + new service and adds iptables rules to capture traffic to the Service port and redirect it to endpoints
6. kube-dns detects there is a new Service and adds the container record to the dns server

questions.question: How to list the endpoints of a certain app?
questions.answer: `kubectl get ep `

questions.question: How can you find out information on a Service related to a certain Pod if all you can use is kubectl exec  --
questions.answer:
You can run `kubectl exec  -- env` which will give you a couple environment variables related to the Service.
Variables such as `[SERVICE_NAME]_SERVICE_HOST`, `[SERVICE_NAME]_SERVICE_PORT`, ...

questions.question: Describe what happens when a container tries to connect with its corresponding Service for the first time. Explain who added each of the components you include in your description
questions.answer:
- The container looks at the nameserver defined in /etc/resolv.conf
  - The container queries the nameserver so the address is resolved to the Service IP
  - Requests sent to the Service IP are forwarded with iptables rules (or other chosen software) to the endpoint(s).

Explanation as to who added them:

  - The nameserver in the container is added by kubelet during the scheduling of the Pod, by using kube-dns
  - The DNS record of the service is added by kube-dns during the Service creation
  - iptables rules are added by kube-proxy during Endpoint and Service creation

questions.question: Describe in high level what happens when you run kubctl expose deployment remo --type=LoadBalancer --port 8080
questions.answer:
1. Kubectl sends a request to Kubernetes API to create a Service object
2. Kubernetes asks the cloud provider (e.g. AWS, GCP, Azure) to provision a load balancer
3. The newly created load balancer forwards incoming traffic to relevant worker node(s) which forwards the traffic to the relevant containers

questions.question: After creating a service that forwards incoming external traffic to the containerized application, how to make sure it works?
questions.answer: You can run `curl :` to examine the output.

questions.question: An internal load balancer in Kubernetes is called ____ and an external load balancer is called ____
questions.answer: An internal load balancer in Kubernetes is called Service and an external load balancer is Ingress

questions.question: What is Ingress?
questions.answer:
From Kubernetes docs: "Ingress exposes HTTP and HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services within the cluster. Traffic routing is controlled by rules defined on the Ingress resource."

Read more [here](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/)

questions.question: Complete the following configuration file to make it Ingress  ``` metadata:   name: someapp-ingress spec: ```
questions.answer:
There are several ways to answer this question.

```
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: someapp-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: my.host
    http:
      paths:
      - backend:
          serviceName: someapp-internal-service
          servicePort: 8080
```

questions.question: Explain the meaning of "http", "host" and "backend" directives  ``` apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata:   name: someapp-ingress spec:   rules:   - host: my.host     http:       paths:       - backend:           serviceName: someapp-internal-service           servicePort: 8080 ```
questions.answer:
host is the entry point of the cluster so basically a valid domain address that maps to cluster's node IP address
the http line used for specifying that incoming requests will be forwarded to the internal service using http.
backend is referencing the internal service (serviceName is the name under metadata and servicePort is the port under the ports section).

questions.question: Why using a wildcard in ingress host may lead to issues?
questions.answer: The reason you should not wildcard value in a host (like `- host: *`) is because you basically tell your Kubernetes cluster to forward all the traffic to the container where you used this ingress. This may cause the entire cluster to go down.

questions.question: What is Ingress Controller?
questions.answer:
An implementation for Ingress. It's basically another pod (or set of pods) that does evaluates and processes Ingress rules and this it manages all the redirections. 

There are multiple Ingress Controller implementations (the one from Kubernetes is Kubernetes Nginx Ingress Controller).

questions.question: What are some use cases for using Ingress?
questions.answer:
* Multiple sub-domains (multiple host entries, each with its own service)
* One domain with multiple services (multiple paths where each one is mapped to a different service/application)

questions.question: How to list Ingress in your namespace?
questions.answer: kubectl get ingress

questions.question: What is Ingress Default Backend?
questions.answer: It specifies what do with an incoming request to the Kubernetes cluster that isn't mapped to any backend (= no rule to for mapping the request to a service). If the default backend service isn't defined, it's recommended to define so users still see some kind of message instead of nothing or unclear error.

questions.question: How to configure a default backend?
questions.answer: Create Service resource that specifies the name of the default backend as reflected in `kubectl describe ingress ...` and the port under the ports section.

questions.question: How to configure TLS with Ingress?
questions.answer:
Add tls and secretName entries.

```
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - some_app.com
    secretName: someapp-secret-tls
```

questions.question: True or False? When configuring Ingress with TLS, the Secret component must be in the same namespace as the Ingress component
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Which Kubernetes concept would you use to control traffic flow at the IP address or port level?
questions.answer: Network Policies

questions.question: How to scale an application (deplyoment) so it runs more than one instance of the application?
questions.answer:
To run two instances of the applicaation?

`kubectl scale deployment  --replicas=2`

You can specify any other number, given that your application knows how to scale.

questions.question: What is the purpose of ReplicaSet?
questions.answer:
[kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/replicaset): "A ReplicaSet's purpose is to maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time. As such, it is often used to guarantee the availability of a specified number of identical Pods."

In simpler words, a ReplicaSet will ensure the specified number of Pods replicas is running for a selected Pod. If there are more Pods than defined in the ReplicaSet, some will be removed. If there are less than what is defined in the ReplicaSet then, then more replicas will be added.

questions.question: What the following block of lines does?  ``` spec:   replicas: 2   selector:     matchLabels:       type: backend   template:     metadata:       labels:         type: backend     spec:       containers:       - name: httpd-yup         image: httpd ```
questions.answer: It defines a replicaset for Pods whose type is set to "backend" so at any given point of time there will be 2 concurrent Pods running.

questions.question: What will happen when a Pod, created by ReplicaSet, is deleted directly with kubectl delete po ...?
questions.answer: The ReplicaSet will create a new Pod in order to reach the desired number of replicas.

questions.question: True or False? If a ReplicaSet defines 2 replicas but there 3 Pods running matching the ReplicaSet selector, it will do nothing
questions.answer: False. It will terminate one of the Pods to reach the desired state of 2 replicas.

questions.question: Describe the sequence of events in case of creating a ReplicaSet
questions.answer:
* The client (e.g. kubectl) sends a request to the API server to create a ReplicaSet
* The Controller detects there is a new event requesting for a ReplicaSet
* The controller creates new Pod definitions (the exact number depends on what is defined in the ReplicaSet definition)
* The scheduler detects unassigned Pods and decides to which nodes to assign the Pods. This information sent to the API server
* Kubelet detects that two Pods were assigned to the node it's running on (as it constantly watching the API server)
* Kubelet sends requests to the container engine, to create the containers that are part of the Pod
* Kubelet sends a request to the API server to notify it the Pods were created

questions.question: How to list ReplicaSets in the current namespace?
questions.answer: `kubectl get rs`

questions.question: Is it possible to delete ReplicaSet without deleting the Pods it created?
questions.answer:
Yes, with `--cascase=false`.

`kubectl delete -f rs.yaml --cascade=false`

questions.question: What is the default number of replicas if not explicitly specified?
What the following output of kubectl get rs means?  NAME                    DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE web                     2         2         0       2m23s
questions.answer: The replicaset `web` has 2 replicas. It seems that the containers inside the Pod(s) are not yet running since the value of READY is 0. It might be normal since it takes time for some containers to start running and it might be due to an error. Running `kubectl describe po POD_NAME` or `kubectl logs POD_NAME` can give us more information.

questions.question: True or False? Pods specified by the selector field of ReplicaSet must be created by the ReplicaSet itself
questions.answer: False. The Pods can be already running and initially they can be created by any object. It doesn't matter for the ReplicaSet and not a requirement for it to acquire and monitor them.

questions.question: True or False? In case of a ReplicaSet, if Pods specified in the selector field don't exists, the ReplicaSet will wait for them to run before doing anything
questions.answer: False. It will take care of running the missing Pods.

questions.question: In case of a ReplicaSet, Which field is mandatory in the spec section?
questions.answer: The field `template` in spec section is mandatory. It's used by the ReplicaSet to create new Pods when needed.

questions.question: You've created a ReplicaSet, how to check whether the ReplicaSet found matching Pods or it created new Pods?
questions.answer:
`kubectl describe rs `

It will be visible under `Events` (the very last lines)

questions.question: True or False? Deleting a ReplicaSet will delete the Pods it created
questions.answer: True (and not only the Pods but anything else it created).

questions.question: True or False? Removing the label from a Pod that is tracked by a ReplicaSet, will cause the ReplicaSet to create a new Pod
questions.answer: True. When the label, used by a ReplicaSet in the selector field, removed from a Pod, that Pod no longer controlled by the ReplicaSet and the ReplicaSet will create a new Pod to compensate for the one it "lost".

questions.question: How to scale a deployment to 8 replicas?
questions.answer: kubectl scale deploy  --replicas=8

questions.question: ReplicaSets are running the moment the user executed the command to create them (like kubectl create -f rs.yaml)
questions.answer: False. It can take some time, depends on what exactly you are running. To see if they are up and running, run `kubectl get rs` and watch the 'READY' column.

questions.question: How to expose a ReplicaSet as a new service?
questions.answer:
`kubectl expose rs  --name= --target-port= --type=NodePort`

Few notes:
  - the target port depends on which port the app is using in the container
  - type can be different and doesn't has to be specifically "NodePort"

questions.question: Fix the following ReplicaSet definition  ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: ReplicaCet metadata:   name: redis   labels:     app: redis     tier: cache spec:   selector:     matchLabels:       tier: cache   template:     metadata:       labels:         tier: cachy     spec:       containers:       - name: redis         image: redis ```
questions.answer: kind should be ReplicaSet and not ReplicaCet :)

questions.question: Fix the following ReplicaSet definition  ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: ReplicaSet metadata:   name: redis   labels:     app: redis     tier: cache spec:   selector:     matchLabels:       tier: cache   template:     metadata:       labels:         tier: cachy     spec:       containers:       - name: redis         image: redis ```
questions.answer: The selector doesn't match the label (cache vs cachy). To solve it, fix cachy so it's cache instead.

questions.question: How to check which container image was used as part of replica set called "repli"?
questions.answer: `k describe rs repli | grep -i image`

questions.question: How to check how many Pods are ready as part of a replica set called "repli"?
questions.answer: `k describe rs repli | grep -i "Pods Status"`

questions.question: How to delete a replica set called "rori"?
questions.answer: `k delete rs rori`

questions.question: How to modify a replica set called "rori" to use a different image?
questions.answer: `k edis rs rori`

questions.question: Scale up a replica set called "rori" to run 5 Pods instead of 2
questions.answer: `k scale rs rori --replicas=5`

questions.question: Scale down a replica set called "rori" to run 1 Pod instead of 5
questions.answer: `k scale rs rori --replicas=1`

questions.question: What's a DaemonSet?
questions.answer: [Kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/daemonset): "A DaemonSet ensures that all (or some) Nodes run a copy of a Pod. As nodes are added to the cluster, Pods are added to them. As nodes are removed from the cluster, those Pods are garbage collected. Deleting a DaemonSet will clean up the Pods it created."

questions.question: What's the difference between a ReplicaSet and DaemonSet?
questions.answer:
A ReplicaSet's purpose is to maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time.
A DaemonSet ensures that all Nodes run a copy of a Pod.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using a DaemonSet?
questions.answer:
* Monitoring: You would like to perform monitoring on every node part of cluster. For example datadog pod runs on  every node using a daemonset
* Logging: You would like to having logging set up on every node part of your cluster
* Networking: there is networking component you need on every node for all nodes to communicate between them

questions.question: How DaemonSet works?
questions.answer:
Historically, up 1.12, it was done with NodeName attribute.

Starting 1.12, it's achieved with regular scheduler and node affinity.

questions.question: How to list all daemonsets in the current namespace?
questions.answer: `kubectl get ds`

questions.question: Explain StatefulSet
questions.answer: StatefulSet is the workload API object used to manage stateful applications. Manages the deployment and scaling of a set of Pods, and provides guarantees about the ordering and uniqueness of these Pods.[Learn more](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/statefulset/)

questions.question: What is a volume in regards to Kubernetes?
questions.answer: A directory accessible by the containers inside a certain Pod and containers. The mechanism responsible for creating the directory, managing it, ... mainly depends on the volume type.

questions.question: What volume types are you familiar with?
questions.answer:
* emptyDir: created when a Pod assigned to a node and ceases to exist when the Pod is no longer running on that node
* hostPath: mounts a path from the host itself. Usually not used due to security risks but has multiple use-cases where it's needed like access to some internal host paths (`/sys`, `/var/lib`, etc.)

questions.question: Which problems, volumes in Kubernetes solve?
questions.answer:
1. Sharing files between containers running in the same Pod
2. Storage in containers is ephemeral - it usually doesn't last for long. For example, when a container crashes, you lose all on-disk data. Certain volumes allows to manage such situation by persistent volumes

questions.question: Explain ephemeral volume types vs. persistent volumes in regards to Pods
questions.answer: Ephemeral volume types have the lifetime of a pod as opposed to persistent volumes which exist beyond the lifetime of a Pod.

questions.question: Provide at least one use-case for each of the following volume types:  * emptyDir * hostPath
questions.answer:
* EmptyDir: You need a temporary data that you can afford to lose if the Pod is deleted. For example short-lived data required for one-time operations.
* hostPath: You need access to paths on the host itself (like data from `/sys` or data generated in `/var/lib`)

questions.question: True or False? By default there is no communication between two Pods in two different namespaces
questions.answer:
False. By default two Pods in two different namespaces are able to communicate with each other.

Try it for yourself:

kubectl run test-prod -n prod --image ubuntu -- sleep 2000000000
kubectl run test-dev -n dev --image ubuntu -- sleep 2000000000

`k describe po test-prod -n prod` to get the IP of test-prod Pod.

Access dev Pod: `kubectl exec --stdin --tty test-dev -n dev -- /bin/bash`

And ping the IP of test-prod Pod you get earlier.You'll see that there is communication between the two pods, in two separate namespaces.

questions.question: Explain Network Policies
questions.answer:
[kubernetes.io](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies): "NetworkPolicies are an application-centric construct which allow you to specify how a pod is allowed to communicate with various network "entities"..."

In simpler words, Network Policies specify how pods are allowed/disallowed to communicate with each other and/or other network endpoints.

questions.question: What are some use cases for using Network Policies?
questions.answer:
- Security:  You want to prevent from everyone to communicate with a certain pod for security reasons
  - Controlling network traffic: You would like to deny network flow between two specific nodes

questions.question: True or False? If no network policies are applied to a pod, then no connections to or from it are allowed
questions.answer: False. By default pods are non-isolated.

questions.question: In case of two pods, if there is an egress policy on the source denining traffic and ingress policy on the destination that allows traffic then, traffic will be allowed or denied?
questions.answer: Denied. Both source and destination policies has to allow traffic for it to be allowed.

questions.question: Where Kubernetes cluster stores the cluster state?
questions.answer: etcd

questions.question: What is etcd?
questions.answer:
etcd is an open source distributed key-value store used to hold and manage the critical information that distributed systems need to keep running.

[Read more here](https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/containers/what-is-etcd)

questions.question: True or False? Etcd holds the current status of any kubernetes component
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? The API server is the only component which communicates directly with etcd
questions.answer: True

questions.question: True or False? application data is not stored in etcd
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Why etcd? Why not some SQL or NoSQL database?
questions.answer:
When chosen as the data store etcd was (and still is of course):

* Highly Available - you can deploy multiple nodes
* Fully Replicated - any node in etcd cluster is "primary" node and has full access to the data
* Consistent - reads return latest data
* Secured - supports both TLS and SSL
* Speed - high performance data store (10k writes per sec!)

questions.question: What are namespaces?
questions.answer: Namespaces allow you split your cluster into virtual clusters where you can group your applications in a way that makes sense and is completely separated from the other groups (so you can for example create an app with the same name in two different namespaces)

questions.question: Why to use namespaces? What is the problem with using one default namespace?
questions.answer:
When using the default namespace alone, it becomes hard over time to get an overview of all the applications you manage in your cluster. Namespaces make it easier to organize the applications into groups that makes sense, like a namespace of all the monitoring applications and a namespace for all the security applications, etc.

Namespaces can also be useful for managing Blue/Green environments where each namespace can include a different version of an app and also share resources that are in other namespaces (namespaces like logging, monitoring, etc.).

Another use case for namespaces is one cluster, multiple teams. When multiple teams use the same cluster, they might end up stepping on each others toes. For example if they end up creating an app with the same name it means one of the teams overridden the app of the other team because there can't be too apps in Kubernetes with the same name (in the same namespace).

questions.question: True or False? When a namespace is deleted all resources in that namespace are not deleted but moved to another default namespace
questions.answer: False. When a namespace is deleted, the resources in that namespace are deleted as well.

questions.question: Describe in detail what the following command does kubectl create deployment kubernetes-httpd --image=httpd
questions.answer:
This is an imperative command that creates a Deployment named `kubernetes-httpd` using the Apache HTTPD (`httpd`) container image. Here's what happens step by step:

1. **kubectl** sends a request to the API server to create a Deployment resource
2. The **API server** validates the request and persists it to etcd
3. The **Deployment controller** detects the new Deployment and creates a ReplicaSet
4. The **ReplicaSet controller** detects the new ReplicaSet and creates a Pod
5. The **Scheduler** assigns the Pod to a suitable node
6. The **kubelet** on that node instructs the container runtime to pull the `httpd` image and start the container

**Defaults applied (since not specified):**
- Replicas: 1 (default when not specified)
- Container port: none exposed by default
- Restart policy: Always (inherited from Deployment)
- Update strategy: RollingUpdate with 25% maxSurge and 25% maxUnavailable

**Equivalent declarative YAML:**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: kubernetes-httpd
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: kubernetes-httpd
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: kubernetes-httpd
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: httpd
        image: httpd
```

**Note:** This creates a running Apache server, but it's not accessible from outside the cluster yet ŌĆö you'd need a Service (NodePort/LoadBalancer) or Ingress to expose it.

questions.question: Why to create kind deployment, if pods can be launched with replicaset?
questions.answer:
Because Deployments provide **above and beyond** what a plain ReplicaSet offers. A ReplicaSet only ensures a specified number of Pod replicas are running ŌĆö that's it.

**What Deployments add on top of ReplicaSets:**

| Capability | Deployment | ReplicaSet |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Rolling updates | Ō£ģ Gradual pod replacement | ŌØī Must manually delete/recreate |
| Rollback | Ō£ģ `kubectl rollout undo` | ŌØī No history |
| Update strategy | Ō£ģ RollingUpdate, Recreate | ŌØī None |
| Version history | Ō£ģ Keeps revision history (default 10) | ŌØī None |
| Pause/Resume | Ō£ģ `kubectl rollout pause` | ŌØī None |
| Scaling | Ō£ģ Declarative | Ō£ģ Declarative |

**Real-world analogy:**
- ReplicaSet = "keep 3 identical pods running"
- Deployment = "keep 3 identical pods running, but also manage how we transition from v1.2 to v1.3, keep a history so we can undo, and let me control the rollout speed"

**You should always use Deployments** for stateless workloads. The ReplicaSet is an internal implementation detail ŌĆö Kubernetes creates one automatically when you create a Deployment. You rarely create ReplicaSets directly.

questions.question: How to get list of resources which are not bound to a specific namespace?
questions.answer: kubectl api-resources --namespaced=false

questions.question: How to delete all pods whose status is not "Running"?
questions.answer: kubectl delete pods --field-selector=status.phase!='Running'

questions.question: How to display the resources usages of pods?
questions.answer: kubectl top pod

questions.question: Perhaps a general question but, you suspect one of the pods is having issues, you don't know what exactly. What do you do?
questions.answer:
Start by inspecting the pods status. we can use the command `kubectl get pods` (--all-namespaces for pods in system namespace)

If we see "Error" status, we can keep debugging by running the command `kubectl describe pod [name]`. In case we still don't see anything useful we can try stern for log tailing.

In case we find out there was a temporary issue with the pod or the system, we can try restarting the pod with the following `kubectl scale deployment [name] --replicas=0`

Setting the replicas to 0 will shut down the process. Now start it with `kubectl scale deployment [name] --replicas=1`

questions.question: What happens what pods are using too much memory? (more than its limit)
questions.answer: They become candidates to for termination.

questions.question: Describe how roll-back works
questions.answer:
Rollback reverts a Deployment to a previous revision. Kubernetes keeps a revision history (default: 10 revisions) of the Deployment's Pod template.

**How it works:**
1. Each time you update a Deployment (change image, env vars, etc.), Kubernetes creates a new ReplicaSet and records the revision
2. The old ReplicaSet(s) are scaled down but NOT deleted ŌĆö they serve as rollback targets
3. A rollback simply scales up an old ReplicaSet and scales down the current one

**Commands:**
```bash
# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapp

# View details of a specific revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapp --revision=3

# Rollback to the previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp

# Rollback to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp --to-revision=2

# Watch rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp

# Check which ReplicaSets belong to a Deployment
kubectl get rs -l app=myapp
```

**What gets rolled back:** Only the Pod template (`spec.template`). Rollback does NOT affect:
- Number of replicas (unchanged from current)
- Deployment strategy (unchanged from current)
- Labels/annotations on the Deployment itself

**Under the hood:** Kubernetes just changes the Deployment's Pod template to match the old one and scales the old ReplicaSet back up. It's essentially a reverse rolling update.

**Limitation:** Rollback only works if the old ReplicaSet still exists. If you deleted it or exceeded `revisionHistoryLimit`, rollback is not possible ŌĆö you'd need to redeploy from your Git/manifest source.

questions.question: True or False? Memory is a compressible resource, meaning that when a container reach the memory limit, it will keep running
questions.answer: False. CPU is a compressible resource while memory is a non compressible resource - once a container reached the memory limit, it will be terminated.

questions.question: What is an Operator?
questions.answer:
Explained [here](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/operator)

"Operators are software extensions to Kubernetes that make use of custom resources to manage applications and their components. Operators follow Kubernetes principles, notably the control loop."

In simpler words, you can think about an operator as a custom control loop in Kubernetes.

questions.question: Why do we need Operators?
questions.answer:
The process of managing stateful applications in Kubernetes isn't as straightforward as managing stateless applications where reaching the desired status and upgrades are both handled the same way for every replica. In stateful applications, upgrading each replica might require different handling due to the stateful nature of the app, each replica might be in a different status. As a result, we often need a human operator to manage stateful applications. Kubernetes Operator is suppose to assist with this.

This also help with automating a standard process on multiple Kubernetes clusters

questions.question: What components the Operator consists of?
questions.answer:
1. CRD (Custom Resource Definition) - You are fanmiliar with Kubernetes resources like Deployment, Pod, Service, etc. CRD is also a resource, but one that you or the developer the operator defines.
2. Controller - Custom control loop which runs against the CRD

questions.question: Explain CRD
questions.answer:
CRD is Custom Resource Definitions. It's custom Kubernetes component which extends K8s API.

TODO(abregman): add more info.

questions.question: How Operator works?
questions.answer:
It uses the control loop used by Kubernetes in general. It watches for changes in the application state. The difference is that is uses a custom control loop.

In addition, it also makes use of CRD's (Custom Resources Definitions) so basically it extends Kubernetes API.

questions.question: True or False? Kubernetes Operator used for stateful applications
questions.answer: True

questions.question: Explain what is the OLM (Operator Lifecycle Manager) and what is it used for
questions.answer:
The Operator Lifecycle Manager (OLM) is a component of the Operator Framework that manages the full lifecycle of Kubernetes Operators ŌĆö from installation to upgrades to removal. It extends Kubernetes with declarative Operator management.

**What OLM does:**
1. **Installation** ŌĆö Resolves dependencies and installs Operators from catalogs (like OperatorHub)
2. **Updates** ŌĆö Manages Operator upgrades (manual or automatic approval strategies)
3. **Dependency management** ŌĆö Ensures required CRDs and APIs exist before installing an Operator
4. **RBAC** ŌĆö Automatically manages permissions needed by Operators

**Key resources OLM introduces:**
- `ClusterServiceVersion` (CSV) ŌĆö Defines all metadata for an Operator (version, icon, required CRDs, permissions, install strategy)
- `Subscription` ŌĆö Keeps an Operator up-to-date by watching a catalog for new versions
- `InstallPlan` ŌĆö The computed plan for installing/upgrading an Operator (can require manual approval)
- `OperatorGroup` ŌĆö Defines which namespaces an Operator can watch
- `CatalogSource` ŌĆö A registry of Operators (e.g., Red Hat's OperatorHub, or a custom catalog)

**Why OLM matters:**
- Without OLM, Operators are installed via raw YAML/Helm with no lifecycle management
- OLM provides consistent upgrade paths, dependency resolution, and governance
- Core component of OpenShift 4 and usable on vanilla Kubernetes

Read more: [OLM Architecture](https://olm.operatorframework.io/docs/)

questions.question: What is the Operator Framework?
questions.answer: open source toolkit used to manage k8s native applications, called operators, in an automated and efficient way.

questions.question: What components the Operator Framework consists of?
questions.answer:
1. Operator SDK - allows developers to build operators
2. Operator Lifecycle Manager - helps to install, update and generally manage the lifecycle of all operators
3. Operator Metering - Enables usage reporting for operators that provide specialized services
4.

  * catalog-operator - Resolving and installing ClusterServiceVersions the resource they specify.
  * olm-operator - Deploys applications defined by ClusterServiceVersion resource

A kubeconfig file is a file used to configure access to Kubernetes when used in conjunction with the kubectl commandline tool (or other clients).
Use kubeconfig files to organize information about clusters, users, namespaces, and authentication mechanisms.

questions.answer: Depends on the scope and maturity of the Operator. If it mainly covers installation and upgrades, Helm might be enough. If you want to go for Lifecycle management, insights and auto-pilot, this is where you'd probably use Go.

In other words, you reference a value from a Kubernetes Secret.

questions.question: How to commit secrets to Git and in general how to use encrypted secrets?
questions.answer:
One possible process would be as follows:

1. You create a Kubernetes secret (but don't commit it)
2. You encrypt it using some 3rd party project (.e.g kubeseal)
3. You apply the seald/encrypted secret
4. You commit the the sealed secret to Git
5. You deploy an application that requires the secret and it can be automatically decrypted by using for example a Bitnami Sealed secrets controller

questions.answer: Persistent Volumes allow us to save data so basically they provide storage that doesn't depend on the pod lifecycle.

questions.answer: True

* NFS

questions.question: What is PersistentVolumeClaim?
questions.answer:
A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a **request for storage** by a user. Think of it as a "storage ticket" ŌĆö you request specific size and access mode, and Kubernetes binds it to a matching PersistentVolume (PV).
